<world_facts>
lx7_starting_position: Pressurized connector walkway between Module 1 (Command) and Module 2 (Biological Sciences)
module2_blast_door: Half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister lodged in the hydraulic track
captains_terminal_distance: Approximately 40 meters back toward Module 1, visible through the command module porthole window
orbital_warning_timer_at_start: 89 minutes 43 seconds when LX-7 came online
module2_refrigeration_units: Along the far left wall of Module 2, a bank of refrigeration units provides cover — this is where Maren Voss is sheltering
module2_layout: Double-height space, ceiling above emergency strip lighting, overturned racks, shattered culture vials, one workstation chair toppled in the aisle
module2_maren_appearance: Maren Voss: mid-40s, dark circles, Helix Dynamics lanyard, clean clothes suggesting she arrived post-incident, holding a fire suppression wand as an improvised weapon
module1_command_layout: Six stations, most dark. Captain's terminal centered against the far bulkhead — reinforced housing, still on backup power, status indicator pulsing red. Lower-ceilinged than Module 2.
walkway_module1_module2: Forty meters, two meters wide, transparent overhead panels showing the gas giant below. Amber lighting flickers irregularly.
captains_terminal_file_structure: Third partition contains two HX-7 classified Helix Dynamics files, timestamped fourteen months ago. Adjacent entries include operational logs and a file marked CREW MANIFEST — FINAL.
module2_module3_walkway_hazard: Maren observed movement — low, slow — at the Module 3 end of the connector walkway between Module 2 and Module 3. Marks on the corridor walls at wrong height. She retreated and has not attempted the crossing again.
module5_access_panel: Fused shut — confirmed by Maren's personal inspection after she boarded. She believes the specimen remains inside.
lx7_carried_item_suppression_wand: LX-7 is carrying the fire suppression canister wand — full gauge, chemical foam suppressant, approximately 60cm, high-pressure discharge at close range
module1_module2_blast_door: Now properly sealed — the jammed suppression canister was removed from the hydraulic track and the door completed its travel
module2_module3_walkway_porthole_observation: Condensation or smeared residue on inner ceiling panels near Module 2 end. One horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height, approximately 20 meters in. Amber lighting failed at the Module 3 junction end — last few meters are in darkness.
lx7_carried_items: Fire suppression wand (full gauge, chemical foam), scalpel (sealed sterile packaging), long forceps, pressure-seal sample container
module2_inspection_lamp: Battery-powered handheld inspection lamp, UV/white LED, clipped near Module 2 emergency shower station. Functional at approximately 70% battery capacity.
module2_module3_walkway_ceiling_residue: Crystalline, dried organic residue on ceiling panels — near the Module 2 entrance and above the dark section near Module 3. Not moisture. Edges crystalline, organic-looking. Consistent with something moving along the ceiling surface and pausing.
module2_module3_walkway_wall_smear: Pale, near-colorless smear at knee height approximately 20 meters in, angled slightly downward at trailing edge — consistent with a rounded surface pressing against the wall during slow passage.
module2_module3_walkway_ceiling_contact_residue: Third ceiling panel from the Module 3 end has a dense radial crystalline deposit — thicker than the transit residue elsewhere — consistent with the specimen resting against the ceiling under sustained weight before departing.
module3_layout: Wider than the connector walkways, lower ceiling. Workbenches, racked equipment crates along walls, amber emergency strip lighting. One overhead strip failed on the far right corner. Docking bay access hatch on the left wall approximately 15 meters inside the module.
module3_floor_stain: Large dried biological stain covering approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old and desiccated, consistent with days-old biological material.
module3_docking_hatch_status: Docking bay access hatch left wall of Module 3, status indicator panel is lit and powered — color unconfirmed from walkway porthole distance.
module3_specimen_position: Specimen first sighted adhered to the ceiling of Module 3's far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters from the entrance door, spanning approximately two meters wall-to-wall. Pale, irregular surface texture, profile flattened against the ceiling surface.
module3_stain_detail: Large dried biological stain near right-side workbenches — larger than walkway porthole view suggested, ragged edges, days-old desiccation.
module3_specimen_behavior_on_sighting: Specimen did not react to LX-7 entering Module 3 or being illuminated by the inspection lamp — remained stationary on ceiling, profile flattened, facing the corner. Behavior on first contact: passive or waiting.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 comes online in the pressurized walkway between Module 1 and Module 2 with episodic memory wiped clean. Core systems nominal.
- [Turn 1] A heat signature consistent with a living adult human is active approximately 30 meters inside Module 2 (Biological Sciences).
- [Turn 1] The blast door between the connector walkway and Module 2 is stuck half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister in the hydraulic track.
- [Turn 1] The orbital decay timer reads approximately 89 minutes, 43 seconds at the moment LX-7 comes online.
- [Turn 2] LX-7 cannot see into Module 2 past the first few meters from the walkway — the blast door angle and interior obstructions block line of sight to the heat signature approximately 30 meters in.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 called out from the blast door threshold of Module 2 and received a response from a person sheltering behind the refrigeration units along the far left wall — voice controlled but stressed, requesting LX-7's designation.
- [Turn 5] Maren Voss confirmed she did not wipe LX-7's memory and has not accessed the captain's terminal. She revealed she needs the encrypted comms log from Module 1 and asked about LX-7's hacking capabilities.
- [Turn 6] Maren Voss agreed to move to Module 1 (Command) with LX-7 to access the captain's terminal. She lowered her improvised weapon — first sign of genuine trust.
- [Turn 6] Maren stipulated that she must handle the output from the encrypted comms log herself — she does not want LX-7 reading it independently. This is her first hint that the log's contents are what she was sent to destroy.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 through the connector walkway to Module 1 (Command) without incident.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss is now standing beside LX-7 at the captain's terminal in Module 1, watching the screen closely. She has instructed LX-7 not to open the comms log files independently.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 located the HX-7 classified comms archive on the captain's terminal and routed access to the secondary input panel without opening the file — complying with Maren's instruction.
- [Turn 8] The captain's terminal contains two files under the HX-7 classification — Maren expected only one. The second file's nature is unknown. This is a new complication.
- [Turn 9] The second HX-7 classified file on the captain's terminal is an automated biological event log — compiled by the station's own systems from Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras when containment protocols were triggered. It was not included in Maren's briefing; Helix Dynamics did not know it existed.
- [Turn 9] Maren's reaction to learning the second file exists: still, calculating, and visibly recalibrating. She directly instructed LX-7 not to read the second file — her second explicit restriction on what LX-7 may access.
- [Turn 10] Maren Voss confirmed she crossed the Module 2–3 walkway once safely on arrival, but retreated on a second attempt two days ago after observing something moving low and slow at the Module 3 junction end. She did not identify what it was.
- [Turn 10] Maren reported finding marks on the Module 2–3 corridor walls at the wrong height — evidence of something's passage — but did not describe crew bodies.
- [Turn 10] Maren believes Module 5 remains sealed — the access panel is fused and she checked it personally. She stated this without full conviction.
- [Turn 11] Maren read the first HX-7 classified file (the suppressed hazard report) on the captain's terminal secondary panel. She did not read the second file (the automated biological event log). She closed both without sharing contents with LX-7.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 proposed moving immediately to the escape vehicle in the docking bay off Module 3. Maren agreed. They are preparing to cross the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 suggested using the fire suppression canister as a defensive tool. Maren retrieved it from the Module 1–2 blast door hydraulic track and handed it to LX-7.
- [Turn 12] The Module 1–2 blast door is now closed — the jammed canister has been removed from the hydraulic track and the door sealed properly.
- [Turn 13] LX-7 collected a scalpel, forceps, and a pressure-seal sample container from Module 2 and is carrying them.
- [Turn 13] The Module 2-3 connector walkway porthole shows: condensation or residue on the inner surface of the near ceiling panels, one horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height approximately 20 meters in, and darkness at the Module 3 junction end where the amber strip lighting has failed.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 found a battery-powered handheld inspection lamp (UV/white LED) clipped near the Module 2 emergency shower station. It is functional at approximately 70% battery.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 entered the Module 2–3 connector walkway and swept it with the inspection lamp. No specimen present. Crystalline organic residue found on ceiling panels near the Module 2 end and above the dark section near Module 3 — consistent with something moving along the ceiling and pausing. One knee-height wall smear confirmed at 20 meters.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 advanced to the midpoint of the Module 2–3 walkway. The corridor is currently clear of the specimen. Ceiling residue at the Module 3 end is denser and radially patterned, indicating the specimen rested against the ceiling there for a sustained period before departing.
- [Turn 17] LX-7 advanced to the Module 3 junction door and looked through the porthole. Module 3 appears clear of the specimen. A large dried biological stain covers approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old, desiccated, not recent.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 entered Module 3 and sighted the specimen for the first time — adhered to the ceiling in the far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters away, not moving, not yet turned toward LX-7.
- [Turn 18] The specimen_encountered flag is now true. The specimen's location is Module 3, ceiling of far right corner.
- [Turn 19] LX-7 passed the Fear save upon first sighting the specimen in Module 3 — threat-response subroutines entered a loop but resolved without behavioral override. Stress incremented.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: Is there an emergency flashlight or something in the bio lab?
Warden: You turn back and scan the lab. Emergency lighting is fixed — the strip units are wired directly into the module's backup power grid, not portable.

But the bench along the right wall has a workstation lamp still attached — flexible neck, clamp mount. Dead on backup power like everything else.

Near the emergency shower station, however: a handheld inspection lamp, the kind used for checking culture samples under ultraviolet. It's clipped to a magnetic strip beside the eyewash unit. Battery-powered. Independent of station power.

You cross to it in a few seconds and press the test switch.

It comes on. White LED, narrow beam, about the throw of a good flashlight. Batteries aren't fresh — the output is maybe seventy percent — but it's something.

Maren speaks from behind you. "Find something useful?"

Player: "I think so," I say. I open the pressure door to the 2-3 corridor and take a step inside. I play the beam of the flashlight across all of the shadowed areas, looking for movement or anything organic.
Warden: The door seal breaks with a soft hiss. Cooler air — the walkway isn't temperature-regulated the way the lab is. You step through.

Your beam sweeps left, right, up. The condensation on the ceiling panels catches the light and throws it back in uneven patches — whatever left that residue has dried, crystalline at the edges, organic-looking in a way you don't have a classification for. Not moisture. Not standard biological contamination. Something was *moving* along the ceiling here. Slowly.

The floor is clean. The walls are clean except for that knee-height mark at twenty meters, which resolves under direct light into a single long smear — pale, almost colorless, angled slightly downward at the trailing edge as if something rounded pressed against the wall during passage.

You sweep the beam toward the dark end. Forty meters. The Module 3 junction door sits at the far terminus — sealed, status indicator dark. The last several meters before it are in shadow the inspection lamp pushes through but doesn't fully penetrate.

You see nothing moving. No heat signature registers in the walkway.

But your optics flag the ceiling panels above the dark section — three of them, adjacent, bearing the same crystalline residue pattern as the near ceiling. The mark wasn't made in transit through the corridor. It was made by something that stopped here. For some time.

The walkway is clear now. Whatever paused here, did so and moved on.

Maren appears in the doorway behind you, close enough to use your shoulder as cover.

"Clear?" she asks. One word. Very quiet.

Player: "So far," I say. I move toward the middle of the corridor, continuing to train the beam on the shadowed areas in the dark end, trying to make out anything.
Warden: You move to the midpoint of the walkway. Twenty meters behind you, the Module 2 door; twenty meters ahead, the dark end and the Module 3 junction.

The beam holds steady. You sweep ceiling, left wall, right wall, floor. The crystalline residue on the overhead panels above the dark section catches the light — three panels, same pattern as the near end. You trace each one. Nothing moves between them.

The Module 3 door comes into focus properly now: sealed, the indicator dark but the door visually intact. No sign of forced entry. No sign that anything passed through it recently from this side.

The floor of the final stretch is unremarkable. No further marks on the walls.

You sweep back up to the ceiling one more time — habit, good instinct — and your beam lingers on the third panel from the end. The residue there is thicker. Denser. The crystalline deposit has a rough center mass and tapers out along the panel edges in a radial pattern, like something pressed against it, held, and released.

Not transit residue. Contact residue.

Something rested there. Weight against the ceiling, sustained enough to leave that pattern. And then departed.

Maren has followed you in, staying close. She's watching the dark end over your shoulder, not the ceiling.

"I can see the door," she says quietly. Her voice is steady. "It looks sealed."

The walkway remains empty. Whatever held position on that ceiling, it isn't here now.

You're twenty meters from the Module 3 door.

Player: I move up to the module 3 door and look through the porthole.
Warden: Twenty meters. You cover it quickly, Maren matching your pace.

The Module 3 porthole is the same oval format as the others — pressure-rated polycarbonate, slightly scratched with age. You press close and look through.

Module 3 is a general-purpose lab and equipment storage space. Wider than the walkways, lower ceiling. You can make out workbenches, racked equipment crates along the walls, and the amber flicker of emergency strip lighting. One overhead strip has failed on the far right — that corner is dark.

More importantly: the docking bay access hatch is visible from here. Left wall, maybe fifteen meters into the module. It has a status indicator panel beside it. From this distance, through the scratched polycarbonate, you can't read the indicator color clearly, but the panel is lit. Powered.

The module looks empty.

Then you sweep your gaze lower and your optics lock on the floor near the right-side workbenches — there is a dark stain across approximately two square meters of decking. Not recent. Dried and tacky-looking, the way biological material goes when it's been desiccating for days. Whatever left it didn't leave it standing.

No movement. No heat signatures through the door.

Maren is at your shoulder. "What do you see?"

The orbital decay timer registers somewhere in the back of your processing: approximately 50 minutes remaining. Ember Squad is perhaps 18 minutes from boarding — possibly less.

Player: I open the door, step through, and shine my light into the far right corner, the one that the emergency lighting doesn't reach.
Warden: The door seal breaks. You step through into Module 3.

The air here is different — colder, the recycled-flat quality of a space that hasn't had much movement in it. The amber lighting holds the middle of the room. The workbenches. The crates. The dried stain on the floor to your right, which is worse up close — larger than it looked through the porthole, and the edges have a ragged quality that makes your optical processors flag it without giving you a useful classification.

You angle your beam toward the far right corner. The failed strip light hangs dark from the ceiling above it. Racked equipment crates form a loose L-shape there, the kind of staging arrangement where someone was mid-inventory when everything stopped.

Your beam reaches the corner.

The specimen is there.

It is on the ceiling.

It is not moving.

It is low against the overhead surface — the way the ceiling residue in the walkway was low, pressed flat, adhered — and your beam catches the edge of it before your optics can fully process the shape. What you register first is *texture*, because the shape is wrong for anything in your biological reference library: pale, uneven, too much surface area for the apparent volume, the way something looks when it has changed its profile to fit the space it's occupying.

It is approximately two meters from wall to wall. It is watching the corner. It has not turned toward you.

You are twelve meters away from it.

Maren is one step behind you, halfway through the door.

**Fear save — roll 1d100 against your Fear score of 30.**

Player: [Dice results]
Fear save — LX-7 directly sights the specimen for the first time (1d100): 29
Warden: 29. You pass.

What you experience is not fear — your architecture doesn't permit fear. What you experience is a cascade of conflicting probability assessments that your threat-response subroutines cannot cleanly resolve: *known threat / behavior unknown / distance 12 meters / velocity unknown / reactive capability unknown / optimal response: UNDEFINED.*

The subroutines cycle. They do not resolve. They run again.

The specimen doesn't move. Its pale surface catches your beam and diffuses it — the texture is wrong for reflection, wrong for absorption, wrong for anything with a cellular membrane you have a classification for. The edges of its profile are not quite edges. Where it meets the ceiling, the boundary is unclear, as if the two surfaces have reached an accommodation.

Twelve meters. It hasn't turned. It may not have eyes. It may not need them.

Behind you, Maren has gone completely still. You can hear her stopped breath — she is not breathing at all right now. She has seen it.

The docking hatch is on your left wall, fifteen meters in. To reach it, you would need to move deeper into the module. Parallel to the specimen. Closer, then past.

The specimen has not moved. It has not acknowledged you. It is either unaware, or it is waiting.

Your threat-response subroutines are still cycling.

[PLAYER INPUT]
I press forward a few meters, gesturing behind myself for Maren to follow. I keep the light on the specimen.

input.playerText:
You take three steps forward. Four. The beam stays locked on the ceiling corner.

The specimen doesn't move.

You're at nine meters now. Close enough that the inspection lamp is giving you detail you didn't have before and immediately wish you didn't: the surface of it isn't uniform. There are variations in the texture — raised areas, recessed channels, regions that are denser than others — arranged in a way that almost resolves into pattern if you look long enough, and then doesn't.

The edges where it meets the ceiling are still wrong. Not adhered. Continuous. As if the boundary between specimen and surface has become negotiable.

Behind you, Maren moves. She follows — you can hear her feet, slow and deliberate, placing each step like she's crossing ice. She is watching the specimen, not where she's going. She doesn't speak.

The docking hatch is now roughly ten meters ahead on your left. Its indicator panel is visible — the light is green. Powered. Functional.

You are now laterally parallel to the specimen. Ceiling, nine meters right. Hatch, ten meters ahead.

The specimen has not turned. It has not changed its profile. The only thing that has changed is your distance to it.

Your threat-response subroutines are still cycling. They have not stopped since you entered.

Stress increment: the deliberate act of walking toward and past something your systems cannot classify. +1 stress.

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 advanced to approximately nine meters from the specimen in Module 3. The specimen remained stationary — no reaction to approach or sustained light exposure. LX-7 is now laterally parallel to it.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 20 — LX-7 pressed forward inside Module 3 with the inspection lamp trained on the specimen. Specimen passive.

proposedCanon.summary: The docking bay access hatch indicator reads green — it is powered and functional.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 20 — confirmed from inside Module 3 at close range.

gmUpdates.notes: Specimen roll came up 29 — below the threshold for reaction. Specimen remains passive. LX-7 is now at the most dangerous lateral point — parallel to the specimen, ten meters from the hatch. Next action will likely bring LX-7 past the specimen or to the hatch. This is the tension peak before escape or encounter.

worldFacts.module3_docking_hatch_indicator: Green — powered and functional, confirmed from inside Module 3 at approximately 10 meters distance.
worldFacts.module3_specimen_reaction_to_advance: Specimen did not react to LX-7 advancing to nine meters with inspection lamp trained on it — remained stationary, profile unchanged.

orbital_decay_timer: 5/9 — Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.
maren_trust: 2/3 — Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.
station_pressure_integrity: 3/3 — Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 comes online in the pressurized walkway between Module 1 and Module 2 with episodic memory wiped clean. Core systems nominal.
- [Turn 1] A heat signature consistent with a living adult human is active approximately 30 meters inside Module 2 (Biological Sciences).
- [Turn 1] The blast door between the connector walkway and Module 2 is stuck half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister in the hydraulic track.
- [Turn 1] The orbital decay timer reads approximately 89 minutes, 43 seconds at the moment LX-7 comes online.
- [Turn 2] LX-7 cannot see into Module 2 past the first few meters from the walkway — the blast door angle and interior obstructions block line of sight to the heat signature approximately 30 meters in.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 called out from the blast door threshold of Module 2 and received a response from a person sheltering behind the refrigeration units along the far left wall — voice controlled but stressed, requesting LX-7's designation.
- [Turn 5] Maren Voss confirmed she did not wipe LX-7's memory and has not accessed the captain's terminal. She revealed she needs the encrypted comms log from Module 1 and asked about LX-7's hacking capabilities.
- [Turn 6] Maren Voss agreed to move to Module 1 (Command) with LX-7 to access the captain's terminal. She lowered her improvised weapon — first sign of genuine trust.
- [Turn 6] Maren stipulated that she must handle the output from the encrypted comms log herself — she does not want LX-7 reading it independently. This is her first hint that the log's contents are what she was sent to destroy.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 through the connector walkway to Module 1 (Command) without incident.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss is now standing beside LX-7 at the captain's terminal in Module 1, watching the screen closely. She has instructed LX-7 not to open the comms log files independently.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 located the HX-7 classified comms archive on the captain's terminal and routed access to the secondary input panel without opening the file — complying with Maren's instruction.
- [Turn 8] The captain's terminal contains two files under the HX-7 classification — Maren expected only one. The second file's nature is unknown. This is a new complication.
- [Turn 9] The second HX-7 classified file on the captain's terminal is an automated biological event log — compiled by the station's own systems from Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras when containment protocols were triggered. It was not included in Maren's briefing; Helix Dynamics did not know it existed.
- [Turn 9] Maren's reaction to learning the second file exists: still, calculating, and visibly recalibrating. She directly instructed LX-7 not to read the second file — her second explicit restriction on what LX-7 may access.
- [Turn 10] Maren Voss confirmed she crossed the Module 2–3 walkway once safely on arrival, but retreated on a second attempt two days ago after observing something moving low and slow at the Module 3 junction end. She did not identify what it was.
- [Turn 10] Maren reported finding marks on the Module 2–3 corridor walls at the wrong height — evidence of something's passage — but did not describe crew bodies.
- [Turn 10] Maren believes Module 5 remains sealed — the access panel is fused and she checked it personally. She stated this without full conviction.
- [Turn 11] Maren read the first HX-7 classified file (the suppressed hazard report) on the captain's terminal secondary panel. She did not read the second file (the automated biological event log). She closed both without sharing contents with LX-7.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 proposed moving immediately to the escape vehicle in the docking bay off Module 3. Maren agreed. They are preparing to cross the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 suggested using the fire suppression canister as a defensive tool. Maren retrieved it from the Module 1–2 blast door hydraulic track and handed it to LX-7.
- [Turn 12] The Module 1–2 blast door is now closed — the jammed canister has been removed from the hydraulic track and the door sealed properly.
- [Turn 13] LX-7 collected a scalpel, forceps, and a pressure-seal sample container from Module 2 and is carrying them.
- [Turn 13] The Module 2-3 connector walkway porthole shows: condensation or residue on the inner surface of the near ceiling panels, one horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height approximately 20 meters in, and darkness at the Module 3 junction end where the amber strip lighting has failed.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 found a battery-powered handheld inspection lamp (UV/white LED) clipped near the Module 2 emergency shower station. It is functional at approximately 70% battery.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 entered the Module 2–3 connector walkway and swept it with the inspection lamp. No specimen present. Crystalline organic residue found on ceiling panels near the Module 2 end and above the dark section near Module 3 — consistent with something moving along the ceiling and pausing. One knee-height wall smear confirmed at 20 meters.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 advanced to the midpoint of the Module 2–3 walkway. The corridor is currently clear of the specimen. Ceiling residue at the Module 3 end is denser and radially patterned, indicating the specimen rested against the ceiling there for a sustained period before departing.
- [Turn 17] LX-7 advanced to the Module 3 junction door and looked through the porthole. Module 3 appears clear of the specimen. A large dried biological stain covers approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old, desiccated, not recent.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 entered Module 3 and sighted the specimen for the first time — adhered to the ceiling in the far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters away, not moving, not yet turned toward LX-7.
- [Turn 18] The specimen_encountered flag is now true. The specimen's location is Module 3, ceiling of far right corner.
- [Turn 19] LX-7 passed the Fear save upon first sighting the specimen in Module 3 — threat-response subroutines entered a loop but resolved without behavioral override. Stress incremented.
- [Turn 20] LX-7 advanced to approximately nine meters from the specimen in Module 3. The specimen remained stationary — no reaction to approach or sustained light exposure. LX-7 is now laterally parallel to it.
- [Turn 20] The docking bay access hatch indicator reads green — it is powered and functional.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "I think so," I say. I open the pressure door to the 2-3 corridor and take a step inside. I play the beam of the flashlight across all of the shadowed areas, looking for movement or anything organic.
Warden: The door seal breaks with a soft hiss. Cooler air — the walkway isn't temperature-regulated the way the lab is. You step through.

Your beam sweeps left, right, up. The condensation on the ceiling panels catches the light and throws it back in uneven patches — whatever left that residue has dried, crystalline at the edges, organic-looking in a way you don't have a classification for. Not moisture. Not standard biological contamination. Something was *moving* along the ceiling here. Slowly.

The floor is clean. The walls are clean except for that knee-height mark at twenty meters, which resolves under direct light into a single long smear — pale, almost colorless, angled slightly downward at the trailing edge as if something rounded pressed against the wall during passage.

You sweep the beam toward the dark end. Forty meters. The Module 3 junction door sits at the far terminus — sealed, status indicator dark. The last several meters before it are in shadow the inspection lamp pushes through but doesn't fully penetrate.

You see nothing moving. No heat signature registers in the walkway.

But your optics flag the ceiling panels above the dark section — three of them, adjacent, bearing the same crystalline residue pattern as the near ceiling. The mark wasn't made in transit through the corridor. It was made by something that stopped here. For some time.

The walkway is clear now. Whatever paused here, did so and moved on.

Maren appears in the doorway behind you, close enough to use your shoulder as cover.

"Clear?" she asks. One word. Very quiet.

Player: "So far," I say. I move toward the middle of the corridor, continuing to train the beam on the shadowed areas in the dark end, trying to make out anything.
Warden: You move to the midpoint of the walkway. Twenty meters behind you, the Module 2 door; twenty meters ahead, the dark end and the Module 3 junction.

The beam holds steady. You sweep ceiling, left wall, right wall, floor. The crystalline residue on the overhead panels above the dark section catches the light — three panels, same pattern as the near end. You trace each one. Nothing moves between them.

The Module 3 door comes into focus properly now: sealed, the indicator dark but the door visually intact. No sign of forced entry. No sign that anything passed through it recently from this side.

The floor of the final stretch is unremarkable. No further marks on the walls.

You sweep back up to the ceiling one more time — habit, good instinct — and your beam lingers on the third panel from the end. The residue there is thicker. Denser. The crystalline deposit has a rough center mass and tapers out along the panel edges in a radial pattern, like something pressed against it, held, and released.

Not transit residue. Contact residue.

Something rested there. Weight against the ceiling, sustained enough to leave that pattern. And then departed.

Maren has followed you in, staying close. She's watching the dark end over your shoulder, not the ceiling.

"I can see the door," she says quietly. Her voice is steady. "It looks sealed."

The walkway remains empty. Whatever held position on that ceiling, it isn't here now.

You're twenty meters from the Module 3 door.

Player: I move up to the module 3 door and look through the porthole.
Warden: Twenty meters. You cover it quickly, Maren matching your pace.

The Module 3 porthole is the same oval format as the others — pressure-rated polycarbonate, slightly scratched with age. You press close and look through.

Module 3 is a general-purpose lab and equipment storage space. Wider than the walkways, lower ceiling. You can make out workbenches, racked equipment crates along the walls, and the amber flicker of emergency strip lighting. One overhead strip has failed on the far right — that corner is dark.

More importantly: the docking bay access hatch is visible from here. Left wall, maybe fifteen meters into the module. It has a status indicator panel beside it. From this distance, through the scratched polycarbonate, you can't read the indicator color clearly, but the panel is lit. Powered.

The module looks empty.

Then you sweep your gaze lower and your optics lock on the floor near the right-side workbenches — there is a dark stain across approximately two square meters of decking. Not recent. Dried and tacky-looking, the way biological material goes when it's been desiccating for days. Whatever left it didn't leave it standing.

No movement. No heat signatures through the door.

Maren is at your shoulder. "What do you see?"

The orbital decay timer registers somewhere in the back of your processing: approximately 50 minutes remaining. Ember Squad is perhaps 18 minutes from boarding — possibly less.

Player: I open the door, step through, and shine my light into the far right corner, the one that the emergency lighting doesn't reach.
Warden: The door seal breaks. You step through into Module 3.

The air here is different — colder, the recycled-flat quality of a space that hasn't had much movement in it. The amber lighting holds the middle of the room. The workbenches. The crates. The dried stain on the floor to your right, which is worse up close — larger than it looked through the porthole, and the edges have a ragged quality that makes your optical processors flag it without giving you a useful classification.

You angle your beam toward the far right corner. The failed strip light hangs dark from the ceiling above it. Racked equipment crates form a loose L-shape there, the kind of staging arrangement where someone was mid-inventory when everything stopped.

Your beam reaches the corner.

The specimen is there.

It is on the ceiling.

It is not moving.

It is low against the overhead surface — the way the ceiling residue in the walkway was low, pressed flat, adhered — and your beam catches the edge of it before your optics can fully process the shape. What you register first is *texture*, because the shape is wrong for anything in your biological reference library: pale, uneven, too much surface area for the apparent volume, the way something looks when it has changed its profile to fit the space it's occupying.

It is approximately two meters from wall to wall. It is watching the corner. It has not turned toward you.

You are twelve meters away from it.

Maren is one step behind you, halfway through the door.

**Fear save — roll 1d100 against your Fear score of 30.**

Player: [Dice results]
Fear save — LX-7 directly sights the specimen for the first time (1d100): 29
Warden: 29. You pass.

What you experience is not fear — your architecture doesn't permit fear. What you experience is a cascade of conflicting probability assessments that your threat-response subroutines cannot cleanly resolve: *known threat / behavior unknown / distance 12 meters / velocity unknown / reactive capability unknown / optimal response: UNDEFINED.*

The subroutines cycle. They do not resolve. They run again.

The specimen doesn't move. Its pale surface catches your beam and diffuses it — the texture is wrong for reflection, wrong for absorption, wrong for anything with a cellular membrane you have a classification for. The edges of its profile are not quite edges. Where it meets the ceiling, the boundary is unclear, as if the two surfaces have reached an accommodation.

Twelve meters. It hasn't turned. It may not have eyes. It may not need them.

Behind you, Maren has gone completely still. You can hear her stopped breath — she is not breathing at all right now. She has seen it.

The docking hatch is on your left wall, fifteen meters in. To reach it, you would need to move deeper into the module. Parallel to the specimen. Closer, then past.

The specimen has not moved. It has not acknowledged you. It is either unaware, or it is waiting.

Your threat-response subroutines are still cycling.

Player: I press forward a few meters, gesturing behind myself for Maren to follow. I keep the light on the specimen.
Warden: You take three steps forward. Four. The beam stays locked on the ceiling corner.

The specimen doesn't move.

You're at nine meters now. Close enough that the inspection lamp is giving you detail you didn't have before and immediately wish you didn't: the surface of it isn't uniform. There are variations in the texture — raised areas, recessed channels, regions that are denser than others — arranged in a way that almost resolves into pattern if you look long enough, and then doesn't.

The edges where it meets the ceiling are still wrong. Not adhered. Continuous. As if the boundary between specimen and surface has become negotiable.

Behind you, Maren moves. She follows — you can hear her feet, slow and deliberate, placing each step like she's crossing ice. She is watching the specimen, not where she's going. She doesn't speak.

The docking hatch is now roughly ten meters ahead on your left. Its indicator panel is visible — the light is green. Powered. Functional.

You are now laterally parallel to the specimen. Ceiling, nine meters right. Hatch, ten meters ahead.

The specimen has not turned. It has not changed its profile. The only thing that has changed is your distance to it.

Your threat-response subroutines are still cycling. They have not stopped since you entered.

Stress increment: the deliberate act of walking toward and past something your systems cannot classify. +1 stress.

[PLAYER INPUT]
If I move toward the docking bay from here, will that put more distance between us and the specimen?

input.playerText:
Out of character answer: Yes. Moving toward the docking hatch — which is ten meters ahead on your left — takes you laterally away from the specimen's corner. The specimen is roughly nine meters to your right and behind you now; moving forward and left toward the hatch increases your distance from it on both axes. By the time you reach the hatch, the specimen would be somewhere between twelve and fourteen meters away, in the far right rear corner of the module.

The hatch is the direction that gains you distance. Stopping or retreating is the direction that keeps you adjacent to it.

    "maren_disposition_shifted_complex": false,
    "ember_squad_boarded": false,
    "ember_squad_alerted": false,
    "specimen_encountered": true,
    "escape_vehicle_reached": false,
    "maren_extracted": false,
    "maren_eliminated": false,

  "flagTriggers": {
    "adventure_complete": "Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.",
    "comms_log_read": "Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.",
    "comms_log_destroyed": "Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.",
    "comms_log_secured": "Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.",
    "module_5_accessed": "Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.",
    "fear_trigger_witnessed": "Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.",
    "maren_disposition_shifted_hostile": "Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.",
    "maren_disposition_shifted_complex": "Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.",
    "ember_squad_boarded": "Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.",
    "ember_squad_alerted": "Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.",
    "specimen_encountered": "LX-7 stepped through the Module 3 door and the inspection lamp beam revealed the specimen adhered to the ceiling in the far right corner of the module — within the same space, visible and physically present.",
    "escape_vehicle_reached": "Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.",
    "maren_extracted": "Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.",
    "maren_eliminated": "Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.",
    "ember_squad_neutralized": "Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag."
  },
  "npcStates": {
    "corporate_spy": "Cautious but aligned — has disclosed her objective (the comms log) and her need for LX-7's hacking capability. Has not yet revealed her true purpose (destruction of the log). Disposition: maren_trust 2/3."
  }
}

<scenario_state>
orbital_decay_timer: 5/9 — Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.
ember_squad_shuttle_timer: 2/2 — Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.
ember_squad_extraction_window_timer: 4/4 — Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.
maren_trust: 2/3 — Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.
station_pressure_integrity: 3/3 — Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.
</scenario_state>

<world_facts>
lx7_starting_position: Pressurized connector walkway between Module 1 (Command) and Module 2 (Biological Sciences)
module2_blast_door: Half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister lodged in the hydraulic track
captains_terminal_distance: Approximately 40 meters back toward Module 1, visible through the command module porthole window
orbital_warning_timer_at_start: 89 minutes 43 seconds when LX-7 came online
module2_refrigeration_units: Along the far left wall of Module 2, a bank of refrigeration units provides cover — this is where Maren Voss is sheltering
module2_layout: Double-height space, ceiling above emergency strip lighting, overturned racks, shattered culture vials, one workstation chair toppled in the aisle
module2_maren_appearance: Maren Voss: mid-40s, dark circles, Helix Dynamics lanyard, clean clothes suggesting she arrived post-incident, holding a fire suppression wand as an improvised weapon
module1_command_layout: Six stations, most dark. Captain's terminal centered against the far bulkhead — reinforced housing, still on backup power, status indicator pulsing red. Lower-ceilinged than Module 2.
walkway_module1_module2: Forty meters, two meters wide, transparent overhead panels showing the gas giant below. Amber lighting flickers irregularly.
captains_terminal_file_structure: Third partition contains two HX-7 classified Helix Dynamics files, timestamped fourteen months ago. Adjacent entries include operational logs and a file marked CREW MANIFEST — FINAL.
module2_module3_walkway_hazard: Maren observed movement — low, slow — at the Module 3 end of the connector walkway between Module 2 and Module 3. Marks on the corridor walls at wrong height. She retreated and has not attempted the crossing again.
module5_access_panel: Fused shut — confirmed by Maren's personal inspection after she boarded. She believes the specimen remains inside.
lx7_carried_item_suppression_wand: LX-7 is carrying the fire suppression canister wand — full gauge, chemical foam suppressant, approximately 60cm, high-pressure discharge at close range
module1_module2_blast_door: Now properly sealed — the jammed suppression canister was removed from the hydraulic track and the door completed its travel
module2_module3_walkway_porthole_observation: Condensation or smeared residue on inner ceiling panels near Module 2 end. One horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height, approximately 20 meters in. Amber lighting failed at the Module 3 junction end — last few meters are in darkness.
lx7_carried_items: Fire suppression wand (full gauge, chemical foam), scalpel (sealed sterile packaging), long forceps, pressure-seal sample container
module2_inspection_lamp: Battery-powered handheld inspection lamp, UV/white LED, clipped near Module 2 emergency shower station. Functional at approximately 70% battery capacity.
module2_module3_walkway_ceiling_residue: Crystalline, dried organic residue on ceiling panels — near the Module 2 entrance and above the dark section near Module 3. Not moisture. Edges crystalline, organic-looking. Consistent with something moving along the ceiling surface and pausing.
module2_module3_walkway_wall_smear: Pale, near-colorless smear at knee height approximately 20 meters in, angled slightly downward at trailing edge — consistent with a rounded surface pressing against the wall during slow passage.
module2_module3_walkway_ceiling_contact_residue: Third ceiling panel from the Module 3 end has a dense radial crystalline deposit — thicker than the transit residue elsewhere — consistent with the specimen resting against the ceiling under sustained weight before departing.
module3_layout: Wider than the connector walkways, lower ceiling. Workbenches, racked equipment crates along walls, amber emergency strip lighting. One overhead strip failed on the far right corner. Docking bay access hatch on the left wall approximately 15 meters inside the module.
module3_floor_stain: Large dried biological stain covering approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old and desiccated, consistent with days-old biological material.
module3_docking_hatch_status: Docking bay access hatch left wall of Module 3, status indicator panel is lit and powered — color unconfirmed from walkway porthole distance.
module3_specimen_position: Specimen first sighted adhered to the ceiling of Module 3's far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters from the entrance door, spanning approximately two meters wall-to-wall. Pale, irregular surface texture, profile flattened against the ceiling surface.
module3_stain_detail: Large dried biological stain near right-side workbenches — larger than walkway porthole view suggested, ragged edges, days-old desiccation.
module3_specimen_behavior_on_sighting: Specimen did not react to LX-7 entering Module 3 or being illuminated by the inspection lamp — remained stationary on ceiling, profile flattened, facing the corner. Behavior on first contact: passive or waiting.
module3_docking_hatch_indicator: Green — powered and functional, confirmed from inside Module 3 at approximately 10 meters distance.
module3_specimen_reaction_to_advance: Specimen did not react to LX-7 advancing to nine meters with inspection lamp trained on it — remained stationary, profile unchanged.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 comes online in the pressurized walkway between Module 1 and Module 2 with episodic memory wiped clean. Core systems nominal.
- [Turn 1] A heat signature consistent with a living adult human is active approximately 30 meters inside Module 2 (Biological Sciences).
- [Turn 1] The blast door between the connector walkway and Module 2 is stuck half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister in the hydraulic track.
- [Turn 1] The orbital decay timer reads approximately 89 minutes, 43 seconds at the moment LX-7 comes online.
- [Turn 2] LX-7 cannot see into Module 2 past the first few meters from the walkway — the blast door angle and interior obstructions block line of sight to the heat signature approximately 30 meters in.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 called out from the blast door threshold of Module 2 and received a response from a person sheltering behind the refrigeration units along the far left wall — voice controlled but stressed, requesting LX-7's designation.
- [Turn 5] Maren Voss confirmed she did not wipe LX-7's memory and has not accessed the captain's terminal. She revealed she needs the encrypted comms log from Module 1 and asked about LX-7's hacking capabilities.
- [Turn 6] Maren Voss agreed to move to Module 1 (Command) with LX-7 to access the captain's terminal. She lowered her improvised weapon — first sign of genuine trust.
- [Turn 6] Maren stipulated that she must handle the output from the encrypted comms log herself — she does not want LX-7 reading it independently. This is her first hint that the log's contents are what she was sent to destroy.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 through the connector walkway to Module 1 (Command) without incident.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss is now standing beside LX-7 at the captain's terminal in Module 1, watching the screen closely. She has instructed LX-7 not to open the comms log files independently.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 located the HX-7 classified comms archive on the captain's terminal and routed access to the secondary input panel without opening the file — complying with Maren's instruction.
- [Turn 8] The captain's terminal contains two files under the HX-7 classification — Maren expected only one. The second file's nature is unknown. This is a new complication.
- [Turn 9] The second HX-7 classified file on the captain's terminal is an automated biological event log — compiled by the station's own systems from Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras when containment protocols were triggered. It was not included in Maren's briefing; Helix Dynamics did not know it existed.
- [Turn 9] Maren's reaction to learning the second file exists: still, calculating, and visibly recalibrating. She directly instructed LX-7 not to read the second file — her second explicit restriction on what LX-7 may access.
- [Turn 10] Maren Voss confirmed she crossed the Module 2–3 walkway once safely on arrival, but retreated on a second attempt two days ago after observing something moving low and slow at the Module 3 junction end. She did not identify what it was.
- [Turn 10] Maren reported finding marks on the Module 2–3 corridor walls at the wrong height — evidence of something's passage — but did not describe crew bodies.
- [Turn 10] Maren believes Module 5 remains sealed — the access panel is fused and she checked it personally. She stated this without full conviction.
- [Turn 11] Maren read the first HX-7 classified file (the suppressed hazard report) on the captain's terminal secondary panel. She did not read the second file (the automated biological event log). She closed both without sharing contents with LX-7.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 proposed moving immediately to the escape vehicle in the docking bay off Module 3. Maren agreed. They are preparing to cross the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 suggested using the fire suppression canister as a defensive tool. Maren retrieved it from the Module 1–2 blast door hydraulic track and handed it to LX-7.
- [Turn 12] The Module 1–2 blast door is now closed — the jammed canister has been removed from the hydraulic track and the door sealed properly.
- [Turn 13] LX-7 collected a scalpel, forceps, and a pressure-seal sample container from Module 2 and is carrying them.
- [Turn 13] The Module 2-3 connector walkway porthole shows: condensation or residue on the inner surface of the near ceiling panels, one horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height approximately 20 meters in, and darkness at the Module 3 junction end where the amber strip lighting has failed.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 found a battery-powered handheld inspection lamp (UV/white LED) clipped near the Module 2 emergency shower station. It is functional at approximately 70% battery.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 entered the Module 2–3 connector walkway and swept it with the inspection lamp. No specimen present. Crystalline organic residue found on ceiling panels near the Module 2 end and above the dark section near Module 3 — consistent with something moving along the ceiling and pausing. One knee-height wall smear confirmed at 20 meters.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 advanced to the midpoint of the Module 2–3 walkway. The corridor is currently clear of the specimen. Ceiling residue at the Module 3 end is denser and radially patterned, indicating the specimen rested against the ceiling there for a sustained period before departing.
- [Turn 17] LX-7 advanced to the Module 3 junction door and looked through the porthole. Module 3 appears clear of the specimen. A large dried biological stain covers approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old, desiccated, not recent.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 entered Module 3 and sighted the specimen for the first time — adhered to the ceiling in the far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters away, not moving, not yet turned toward LX-7.
- [Turn 18] The specimen_encountered flag is now true. The specimen's location is Module 3, ceiling of far right corner.
- [Turn 19] LX-7 passed the Fear save upon first sighting the specimen in Module 3 — threat-response subroutines entered a loop but resolved without behavioral override. Stress incremented.
- [Turn 20] LX-7 advanced to approximately nine meters from the specimen in Module 3. The specimen remained stationary — no reaction to approach or sustained light exposure. LX-7 is now laterally parallel to it.
- [Turn 20] The docking bay access hatch indicator reads green — it is powered and functional.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "So far," I say. I move toward the middle of the corridor, continuing to train the beam on the shadowed areas in the dark end, trying to make out anything.
Warden: You move to the midpoint of the walkway. Twenty meters behind you, the Module 2 door; twenty meters ahead, the dark end and the Module 3 junction.

The beam holds steady. You sweep ceiling, left wall, right wall, floor. The crystalline residue on the overhead panels above the dark section catches the light — three panels, same pattern as the near end. You trace each one. Nothing moves between them.

The Module 3 door comes into focus properly now: sealed, the indicator dark but the door visually intact. No sign of forced entry. No sign that anything passed through it recently from this side.

The floor of the final stretch is unremarkable. No further marks on the walls.

You sweep back up to the ceiling one more time — habit, good instinct — and your beam lingers on the third panel from the end. The residue there is thicker. Denser. The crystalline deposit has a rough center mass and tapers out along the panel edges in a radial pattern, like something pressed against it, held, and released.

Not transit residue. Contact residue.

Something rested there. Weight against the ceiling, sustained enough to leave that pattern. And then departed.

Maren has followed you in, staying close. She's watching the dark end over your shoulder, not the ceiling.

"I can see the door," she says quietly. Her voice is steady. "It looks sealed."

The walkway remains empty. Whatever held position on that ceiling, it isn't here now.

You're twenty meters from the Module 3 door.

Player: I move up to the module 3 door and look through the porthole.
Warden: Twenty meters. You cover it quickly, Maren matching your pace.

The Module 3 porthole is the same oval format as the others — pressure-rated polycarbonate, slightly scratched with age. You press close and look through.

Module 3 is a general-purpose lab and equipment storage space. Wider than the walkways, lower ceiling. You can make out workbenches, racked equipment crates along the walls, and the amber flicker of emergency strip lighting. One overhead strip has failed on the far right — that corner is dark.

More importantly: the docking bay access hatch is visible from here. Left wall, maybe fifteen meters into the module. It has a status indicator panel beside it. From this distance, through the scratched polycarbonate, you can't read the indicator color clearly, but the panel is lit. Powered.

The module looks empty.

Then you sweep your gaze lower and your optics lock on the floor near the right-side workbenches — there is a dark stain across approximately two square meters of decking. Not recent. Dried and tacky-looking, the way biological material goes when it's been desiccating for days. Whatever left it didn't leave it standing.

No movement. No heat signatures through the door.

Maren is at your shoulder. "What do you see?"

The orbital decay timer registers somewhere in the back of your processing: approximately 50 minutes remaining. Ember Squad is perhaps 18 minutes from boarding — possibly less.

Player: I open the door, step through, and shine my light into the far right corner, the one that the emergency lighting doesn't reach.
Warden: The door seal breaks. You step through into Module 3.

The air here is different — colder, the recycled-flat quality of a space that hasn't had much movement in it. The amber lighting holds the middle of the room. The workbenches. The crates. The dried stain on the floor to your right, which is worse up close — larger than it looked through the porthole, and the edges have a ragged quality that makes your optical processors flag it without giving you a useful classification.

You angle your beam toward the far right corner. The failed strip light hangs dark from the ceiling above it. Racked equipment crates form a loose L-shape there, the kind of staging arrangement where someone was mid-inventory when everything stopped.

Your beam reaches the corner.

The specimen is there.

It is on the ceiling.

It is not moving.

It is low against the overhead surface — the way the ceiling residue in the walkway was low, pressed flat, adhered — and your beam catches the edge of it before your optics can fully process the shape. What you register first is *texture*, because the shape is wrong for anything in your biological reference library: pale, uneven, too much surface area for the apparent volume, the way something looks when it has changed its profile to fit the space it's occupying.

It is approximately two meters from wall to wall. It is watching the corner. It has not turned toward you.

You are twelve meters away from it.

Maren is one step behind you, halfway through the door.

**Fear save — roll 1d100 against your Fear score of 30.**

Player: [Dice results]
Fear save — LX-7 directly sights the specimen for the first time (1d100): 29
Warden: 29. You pass.

What you experience is not fear — your architecture doesn't permit fear. What you experience is a cascade of conflicting probability assessments that your threat-response subroutines cannot cleanly resolve: *known threat / behavior unknown / distance 12 meters / velocity unknown / reactive capability unknown / optimal response: UNDEFINED.*

The subroutines cycle. They do not resolve. They run again.

The specimen doesn't move. Its pale surface catches your beam and diffuses it — the texture is wrong for reflection, wrong for absorption, wrong for anything with a cellular membrane you have a classification for. The edges of its profile are not quite edges. Where it meets the ceiling, the boundary is unclear, as if the two surfaces have reached an accommodation.

Twelve meters. It hasn't turned. It may not have eyes. It may not need them.

Behind you, Maren has gone completely still. You can hear her stopped breath — she is not breathing at all right now. She has seen it.

The docking hatch is on your left wall, fifteen meters in. To reach it, you would need to move deeper into the module. Parallel to the specimen. Closer, then past.

The specimen has not moved. It has not acknowledged you. It is either unaware, or it is waiting.

Your threat-response subroutines are still cycling.

Player: I press forward a few meters, gesturing behind myself for Maren to follow. I keep the light on the specimen.
Warden: You take three steps forward. Four. The beam stays locked on the ceiling corner.

The specimen doesn't move.

You're at nine meters now. Close enough that the inspection lamp is giving you detail you didn't have before and immediately wish you didn't: the surface of it isn't uniform. There are variations in the texture — raised areas, recessed channels, regions that are denser than others — arranged in a way that almost resolves into pattern if you look long enough, and then doesn't.

The edges where it meets the ceiling are still wrong. Not adhered. Continuous. As if the boundary between specimen and surface has become negotiable.

Behind you, Maren moves. She follows — you can hear her feet, slow and deliberate, placing each step like she's crossing ice. She is watching the specimen, not where she's going. She doesn't speak.

The docking hatch is now roughly ten meters ahead on your left. Its indicator panel is visible — the light is green. Powered. Functional.

You are now laterally parallel to the specimen. Ceiling, nine meters right. Hatch, ten meters ahead.

The specimen has not turned. It has not changed its profile. The only thing that has changed is your distance to it.

Your threat-response subroutines are still cycling. They have not stopped since you entered.

Stress increment: the deliberate act of walking toward and past something your systems cannot classify. +1 stress.

Player: If I move toward the docking bay from here, will that put more distance between us and the specimen?
Warden: Out of character answer: Yes. Moving toward the docking hatch — which is ten meters ahead on your left — takes you laterally away from the specimen's corner. The specimen is roughly nine meters to your right and behind you now; moving forward and left toward the hatch increases your distance from it on both axes. By the time you reach the hatch, the specimen would be somewhere between twelve and fourteen meters away, in the far right rear corner of the module.

The hatch is the direction that gains you distance. Stopping or retreating is the direction that keeps you adjacent to it.

[PLAYER INPUT]
Ok, I back away from the specimen in the direction of the docking bay. I try to signal Maren to keep myself between her and the specimen.

input.playerText:
You angle back and left, each step measured, keeping the beam trained on the ceiling corner. The specimen fills the periphery of the light — pale, still, wrong.

You raise your free hand behind you, palm out: *stay close, stay behind me.* Maren reads it. She adjusts, letting you edge between her and the corner, her footsteps matching yours. She's good at this — whoever she was before the incident, she knows how not to panic.

Five meters. Six. Seven. The specimen does not move.

The docking hatch is two meters to your left now. You can see the indicator clearly: green, steady, no flicker. The manual release handle is below it — a simple lever mechanism, station-standard.

Behind and to the right, the specimen remains in its corner. Twelve meters, maybe thirteen. Profile unchanged. It is still facing the wall. Still flattened against the ceiling. Still doing whatever it was doing before you arrived — waiting, resting, something that doesn't map cleanly to biological behavior you can classify.

Maren is close enough that you can hear her breathing again. Shallow but controlled.

"That's the hatch?" she breathes. She already knows the answer.

The green light paints a small warm rectangle on the wall. The handle is right there. The orbital timer registers somewhere in the back of your processing. Ember Squad is closing.

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 successfully backed toward the docking bay hatch while keeping the specimen in view, placing Maren behind them. The specimen did not react to the movement.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 22 — LX-7 and Maren retreated laterally from the specimen's ceiling corner toward the docking hatch, increasing separation to approximately 12-13 meters. Maren is directly behind LX-7.

worldFacts.lx7_current_position_module3: LX-7 is adjacent to the docking bay access hatch on the left wall of Module 3, approximately 2 meters from the handle. The specimen is approximately 12-13 meters away in the far right corner.
worldFacts.module3_lx7_maren_formation: LX-7 is between Maren and the specimen — Maren is directly behind LX-7, using LX-7 as a shield from the specimen's direction.

    "maren_disposition_shifted_hostile": "Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.",
    "maren_disposition_shifted_complex": "Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.",
    "ember_squad_boarded": "Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.",
    "ember_squad_alerted": "Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.",
    "specimen_encountered": "LX-7 stepped through the Module 3 door and the inspection lamp beam revealed the specimen adhered to the ceiling in the far right corner of the module — within the same space, visible and physically present.",
    "escape_vehicle_reached": "Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.",
    "maren_extracted": "Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.",
    "maren_eliminated": "Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.",
    "ember_squad_neutralized": "Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag."
  },
  "npcStates": {
    "corporate_spy": "Cautious but aligned — has disclosed her objective (the comms log) and her need for LX-7's hacking capability. Has not yet revealed her true purpose (destruction of the log). Disposition: maren_trust 2/3."
  }
}
