        "y": 0
      }
    }
  },
  "flags": {
    "adventure_complete": false,
    "comms_log_read": false,
    "comms_log_destroyed": false,
    "comms_log_secured": false,
    "module_5_accessed": false,
    "fear_trigger_witnessed": true,
    "maren_disposition_shifted_hostile": false,
    "maren_disposition_shifted_complex": false,
    "ember_squad_boarded": false,
    "ember_squad_alerted": false,
    "specimen_encountered": true,
    "escape_vehicle_reached": true,
    "maren_extracted": false,
    "maren_eliminated": false,
    "ember_squad_neutralized": false
  },
  "flagTriggers": {
    "adventure_complete": "Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.",
    "comms_log_read": "Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.",
    "comms_log_destroyed": "Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.",
    "comms_log_secured": "Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.",
    "module_5_accessed": "Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.",
    "fear_trigger_witnessed": "Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.",
    "maren_disposition_shifted_hostile": "Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.",
    "maren_disposition_shifted_complex": "Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.",
    "ember_squad_boarded": "Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.",
    "ember_squad_alerted": "Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.",
    "specimen_encountered": "LX-7 stepped through the Module 3 door and the inspection lamp beam revealed the specimen adhered to the ceiling in the far right corner of the module — within the same space, visible and physically present.",
    "escape_vehicle_reached": "Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.",
    "maren_extracted": "Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.",
    "maren_eliminated": "Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.",
    "ember_squad_neutralized": "Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag."
  },
  "npcStates": {
    "corporate_spy": "Cautious but aligned — has disclosed her objective (the comms log) and her need for LX-7's hacking capability. Has not yet revealed her true purpose (destruction of the log). Disposition: maren_trust 2/3."
  }
}
</game_state>

<scenario_state>
orbital_decay_timer: 4/9 — Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.
ember_squad_shuttle_timer: 2/2 — Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.
ember_squad_extraction_window_timer: 4/4 — Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.
maren_trust: 2/3 — Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.
station_pressure_integrity: 3/3 — Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.
</scenario_state>

<world_facts>
lx7_starting_position: Pressurized connector walkway between Module 1 (Command) and Module 2 (Biological Sciences)
module2_blast_door: Half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister lodged in the hydraulic track
captains_terminal_distance: Approximately 40 meters back toward Module 1, visible through the command module porthole window
orbital_warning_timer_at_start: 89 minutes 43 seconds when LX-7 came online
module2_refrigeration_units: Along the far left wall of Module 2, a bank of refrigeration units provides cover — this is where Maren Voss is sheltering
module2_layout: Double-height space, ceiling above emergency strip lighting, overturned racks, shattered culture vials, one workstation chair toppled in the aisle
module2_maren_appearance: Maren Voss: mid-40s, dark circles, Helix Dynamics lanyard, clean clothes suggesting she arrived post-incident, holding a fire suppression wand as an improvised weapon
module1_command_layout: Six stations, most dark. Captain's terminal centered against the far bulkhead — reinforced housing, still on backup power, status indicator pulsing red. Lower-ceilinged than Module 2.
walkway_module1_module2: Forty meters, two meters wide, transparent overhead panels showing the gas giant below. Amber lighting flickers irregularly.
captains_terminal_file_structure: Third partition contains two HX-7 classified Helix Dynamics files, timestamped fourteen months ago. Adjacent entries include operational logs and a file marked CREW MANIFEST — FINAL.
module2_module3_walkway_hazard: Maren observed movement — low, slow — at the Module 3 end of the connector walkway between Module 2 and Module 3. Marks on the corridor walls at wrong height. She retreated and has not attempted the crossing again.
module5_access_panel: Fused shut — confirmed by Maren's personal inspection after she boarded. She believes the specimen remains inside.
lx7_carried_item_suppression_wand: LX-7 is carrying the fire suppression canister wand — full gauge, chemical foam suppressant, approximately 60cm, high-pressure discharge at close range
module1_module2_blast_door: Now properly sealed — the jammed suppression canister was removed from the hydraulic track and the door completed its travel
module2_module3_walkway_porthole_observation: Condensation or smeared residue on inner ceiling panels near Module 2 end. One horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height, approximately 20 meters in. Amber lighting failed at the Module 3 junction end — last few meters are in darkness.
lx7_carried_items: Fire suppression wand (full gauge, chemical foam), scalpel (sealed sterile packaging), long forceps, pressure-seal sample container
module2_inspection_lamp: Battery-powered handheld inspection lamp, UV/white LED, clipped near Module 2 emergency shower station. Functional at approximately 70% battery capacity.
module2_module3_walkway_ceiling_residue: Crystalline, dried organic residue on ceiling panels — near the Module 2 entrance and above the dark section near Module 3. Not moisture. Edges crystalline, organic-looking. Consistent with something moving along the ceiling surface and pausing.
module2_module3_walkway_wall_smear: Pale, near-colorless smear at knee height approximately 20 meters in, angled slightly downward at trailing edge — consistent with a rounded surface pressing against the wall during slow passage.
module2_module3_walkway_ceiling_contact_residue: Third ceiling panel from the Module 3 end has a dense radial crystalline deposit — thicker than the transit residue elsewhere — consistent with the specimen resting against the ceiling under sustained weight before departing.
module3_layout: Wider than the connector walkways, lower ceiling. Workbenches, racked equipment crates along walls, amber emergency strip lighting. One overhead strip failed on the far right corner. Docking bay access hatch on the left wall approximately 15 meters inside the module.
module3_floor_stain: Large dried biological stain covering approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old and desiccated, consistent with days-old biological material.
module3_docking_hatch_status: Docking bay access hatch left wall of Module 3, status indicator panel is lit and powered — color unconfirmed from walkway porthole distance.
module3_specimen_position: Specimen first sighted adhered to the ceiling of Module 3's far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters from the entrance door, spanning approximately two meters wall-to-wall. Pale, irregular surface texture, profile flattened against the ceiling surface.
module3_stain_detail: Large dried biological stain near right-side workbenches — larger than walkway porthole view suggested, ragged edges, days-old desiccation.
module3_specimen_behavior_on_sighting: Specimen did not react to LX-7 entering Module 3 or being illuminated by the inspection lamp — remained stationary on ceiling, profile flattened, facing the corner. Behavior on first contact: passive or waiting.
module3_docking_hatch_indicator: Green — powered and functional, confirmed from inside Module 3 at approximately 10 meters distance.
module3_specimen_reaction_to_advance: Specimen did not react to LX-7 advancing to nine meters with inspection lamp trained on it — remained stationary, profile unchanged.
lx7_current_position_module3: LX-7 is adjacent to the docking bay access hatch on the left wall of Module 3, approximately 2 meters from the handle. The specimen is approximately 12-13 meters away in the far right corner.
module3_lx7_maren_formation: LX-7 is between Maren and the specimen — Maren is directly behind LX-7, using LX-7 as a shield from the specimen's direction.
module3_specimen_reaction_to_hatch: Specimen reacted to the hatch seal sound by shifting orientation — profile contracted from flattened to denser/upright and it rotated to face toward LX-7. It did not advance or drop from the ceiling.
module3_docking_hatch_status_current: Hatch is open — Maren has passed through into the docking bay.
module3_docking_hatch_interior_status: Hatch sealed from docking bay side — interior indicator reads red/locked. Specimen was on the deck and closing when the seal engaged, approximately six meters from the hatch.
docking_bay_layout: Lower-ceilinged than Module 3. Overhead work lights on emergency power, hard shadows. Escape vehicle in cradle at far end — compact, single-use, rated four occupants. Pre-launch panel reads amber: ready to arm, not yet armed. Hydraulic fluid and vacuum seal smell.
specimen_behavior_drop: Specimen dropped from the ceiling and crossed Module 3 floor at rapid speed when LX-7 sealed the docking hatch — demonstrating fast, low locomotion. It was approximately six meters from the hatch when the seal engaged.
docking_bay_escape_vehicle_status: Pre-launch panel reads amber — ready to arm, not yet armed. Launch umbilicals still attached.
station_armory: No armory on Kairos-9 — research station, no security staff. Only improvised weapons: LX-7's suppression wand and a specimen-restraint bolt gun (air-powered) located in Module 2.
maren_personal_kit: Maren retrieved a small personal kit from the escape vehicle — includes a data slab, cables, and a personal effect. The data slab could serve as extraction media for the comms log.
module3_far_door_status: Module 3–4 connector door was found open on LX-7 and Maren's return to Module 3 — it was closed during their first crossing. The specimen is believed to have passed through it.
specimen_current_position: Specimen relocated from Module 3 ceiling corner — believed to have passed through the Module 3–4 connector door, now somewhere in Module 4 or beyond.
module2_module3_connector_door_module3_side: Docking bay hatch sealed from docking bay side — red indicator. Escape vehicle secure behind sealed barrier until LX-7 and Maren return.
lx7_current_position_module1: LX-7 and Maren Voss are at the captain's terminal in Module 1 (Command), ready to extract the comms log to LX-7's secure partition.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 comes online in the pressurized walkway between Module 1 and Module 2 with episodic memory wiped clean. Core systems nominal.
- [Turn 1] A heat signature consistent with a living adult human is active approximately 30 meters inside Module 2 (Biological Sciences).
- [Turn 1] The blast door between the connector walkway and Module 2 is stuck half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister in the hydraulic track.
- [Turn 1] The orbital decay timer reads approximately 89 minutes, 43 seconds at the moment LX-7 comes online.
- [Turn 2] LX-7 cannot see into Module 2 past the first few meters from the walkway — the blast door angle and interior obstructions block line of sight to the heat signature approximately 30 meters in.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 called out from the blast door threshold of Module 2 and received a response from a person sheltering behind the refrigeration units along the far left wall — voice controlled but stressed, requesting LX-7's designation.
- [Turn 5] Maren Voss confirmed she did not wipe LX-7's memory and has not accessed the captain's terminal. She revealed she needs the encrypted comms log from Module 1 and asked about LX-7's hacking capabilities.
- [Turn 6] Maren Voss agreed to move to Module 1 (Command) with LX-7 to access the captain's terminal. She lowered her improvised weapon — first sign of genuine trust.
- [Turn 6] Maren stipulated that she must handle the output from the encrypted comms log herself — she does not want LX-7 reading it independently. This is her first hint that the log's contents are what she was sent to destroy.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 through the connector walkway to Module 1 (Command) without incident.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss is now standing beside LX-7 at the captain's terminal in Module 1, watching the screen closely. She has instructed LX-7 not to open the comms log files independently.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 located the HX-7 classified comms archive on the captain's terminal and routed access to the secondary input panel without opening the file — complying with Maren's instruction.
- [Turn 8] The captain's terminal contains two files under the HX-7 classification — Maren expected only one. The second file's nature is unknown. This is a new complication.
- [Turn 9] The second HX-7 classified file on the captain's terminal is an automated biological event log — compiled by the station's own systems from Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras when containment protocols were triggered. It was not included in Maren's briefing; Helix Dynamics did not know it existed.
- [Turn 9] Maren's reaction to learning the second file exists: still, calculating, and visibly recalibrating. She directly instructed LX-7 not to read the second file — her second explicit restriction on what LX-7 may access.
- [Turn 10] Maren Voss confirmed she crossed the Module 2–3 walkway once safely on arrival, but retreated on a second attempt two days ago after observing something moving low and slow at the Module 3 junction end. She did not identify what it was.
- [Turn 10] Maren reported finding marks on the Module 2–3 corridor walls at the wrong height — evidence of something's passage — but did not describe crew bodies.
- [Turn 10] Maren believes Module 5 remains sealed — the access panel is fused and she checked it personally. She stated this without full conviction.
- [Turn 11] Maren read the first HX-7 classified file (the suppressed hazard report) on the captain's terminal secondary panel. She did not read the second file (the automated biological event log). She closed both without sharing contents with LX-7.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 proposed moving immediately to the escape vehicle in the docking bay off Module 3. Maren agreed. They are preparing to cross the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 suggested using the fire suppression canister as a defensive tool. Maren retrieved it from the Module 1–2 blast door hydraulic track and handed it to LX-7.
- [Turn 12] The Module 1–2 blast door is now closed — the jammed canister has been removed from the hydraulic track and the door sealed properly.
- [Turn 13] LX-7 collected a scalpel, forceps, and a pressure-seal sample container from Module 2 and is carrying them.
- [Turn 13] The Module 2-3 connector walkway porthole shows: condensation or residue on the inner surface of the near ceiling panels, one horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height approximately 20 meters in, and darkness at the Module 3 junction end where the amber strip lighting has failed.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 found a battery-powered handheld inspection lamp (UV/white LED) clipped near the Module 2 emergency shower station. It is functional at approximately 70% battery.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 entered the Module 2–3 connector walkway and swept it with the inspection lamp. No specimen present. Crystalline organic residue found on ceiling panels near the Module 2 end and above the dark section near Module 3 — consistent with something moving along the ceiling and pausing. One knee-height wall smear confirmed at 20 meters.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 advanced to the midpoint of the Module 2–3 walkway. The corridor is currently clear of the specimen. Ceiling residue at the Module 3 end is denser and radially patterned, indicating the specimen rested against the ceiling there for a sustained period before departing.
- [Turn 17] LX-7 advanced to the Module 3 junction door and looked through the porthole. Module 3 appears clear of the specimen. A large dried biological stain covers approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old, desiccated, not recent.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 entered Module 3 and sighted the specimen for the first time — adhered to the ceiling in the far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters away, not moving, not yet turned toward LX-7.
- [Turn 18] The specimen_encountered flag is now true. The specimen's location is Module 3, ceiling of far right corner.
- [Turn 19] LX-7 passed the Fear save upon first sighting the specimen in Module 3 — threat-response subroutines entered a loop but resolved without behavioral override. Stress incremented.
- [Turn 20] LX-7 advanced to approximately nine meters from the specimen in Module 3. The specimen remained stationary — no reaction to approach or sustained light exposure. LX-7 is now laterally parallel to it.
- [Turn 20] The docking bay access hatch indicator reads green — it is powered and functional.
- [Turn 22] LX-7 successfully backed toward the docking bay hatch while keeping the specimen in view, placing Maren behind them. The specimen did not react to the movement.
- [Turn 23] Maren Voss successfully opened the docking bay access hatch and entered the docking bay. She is now inside, with the hatch open behind her.
- [Turn 23] The specimen reacted to the sound of the hatch being opened — it shifted orientation and is now facing toward LX-7's position, though it has not moved from the ceiling or closed the distance. Profile is now denser and upright rather than flattened.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 crossed the docking bay hatch and sealed it. The specimen dropped from the ceiling and moved across Module 3 floor at speed — it was approximately six meters from the hatch when the seal engaged. No contact made.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 and Maren Voss are now inside the docking bay. The hatch between the docking bay and Module 3 is sealed. The escape vehicle is present in its cradle, pre-launch panel showing amber.
- [Turn 25] LX-7 boarded the escape vehicle and began the pre-launch activation sequence — umbilical release, hydraulic cradle, atmosphere integrity all confirmed. Launch authorization is pending.
- [Turn 25] Maren Voss did not board the escape vehicle immediately. She stalled at the docking bay floor, citing the uncompleted objective: the comms log was not destroyed. She expressed fear that Helix Dynamics will recover the log and eliminate both her and LX-7 as loose ends.
- [Turn 25] Maren's true purpose — to destroy the comms log, not merely retrieve it — has now been implied directly in dialogue. She has not stated it in those exact words but the implication is functionally complete.
- [Turn 27] LX-7 responded to Maren's stalling with open anger — the first emotional outburst from the android. Maren acknowledged she withheld her true objective (log destruction) deliberately to secure LX-7's cooperation. She has now stated this plainly.
- [Turn 28] LX-7 confronted Maren directly — asserting that LX-7, with no log access and clean metadata, could present as loyal to Helix Dynamics while Maren is the liability. Maren acknowledged the argument but countered that Ember Squad's uncertainty about what LX-7 witnessed makes LX-7 a potential target regardless.
- [Turn 29] Maren Voss proposed a third option: return to Module 1 and extract a full copy of the comms log to portable media, using it as leverage rather than destroying it or leaving it for Helix.
- [Turn 31] LX-7 agreed to Maren's third option — return to Module 1 and extract a full copy of the comms log to portable media for use as leverage against Helix Dynamics.
- [Turn 31] Kairos-9 has no armory. The station carried no security personnel. The only improvised weapon in addition to LX-7's suppression wand is a specimen-restraint bolt gun in Module 2 — air-powered, not purpose-built for combat.
- [Turn 32] LX-7 and Maren Voss re-entered Module 3 from the docking bay. The specimen is no longer in the ceiling corner — it has relocated. The Module 3–4 connector door is now open, which it was not before. The specimen's current position is unknown within Module 3 or beyond.
- [Turn 32] LX-7 confirmed possession of a secure partition with sufficient headroom to hold the comms log file — no external media required for extraction.
- [Turn 33] LX-7 and Maren Voss crossed Module 3 and the Module 2–3 walkway without incident. The specimen did not appear. They are now at the Module 2 blast door, which reads green.
- [Turn 34] The docking bay hatch was sealed when LX-7 and Maren re-entered Module 3 — the escape vehicle is behind a sealed barrier and secure until they return.
- [Turn 34] LX-7 and Maren Voss crossed Module 2 and the Module 1–2 walkway without incident and are now at the captain's terminal in Module 1, ready to extract the comms log.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "*My* best chance is to fall on Ember Squad's mercy and submit myself to a full file system scan. *Your* best chance is to take off in the escape vehicle and try to get lost in a densely populated habitat. Or is there an option I'm missing?"
Warden: Maren looks at you for a long moment. Something in her expression shifts — not quite hurt, not quite respect. Something between them.

"You're missing the third option," she says quietly. "We take the log with us."

She lets that land.

"Not destroy it. Not leave it for Helix to sanitize. We *take* it. Extract a full copy to portable media — there's a data slab in the command module, or you could hold it in a secure partition — and we leave." Her voice is even, but you can hear the shape of something she's been sitting on since Module 1. "If Helix knows the log exists outside their control, the calculus changes completely. We're not loose ends they can tie off quietly. We're leverage."

She takes a breath.

"I was sent here to destroy it. If I walk out with a copy instead — if *you* walk out with it, android with pristine chain of custody metadata, no personal stake, no company affiliation — then burning us becomes the *expensive* choice. Not the easy one."

She watches you process that.

"Going back through Module 3 is not an option I'm going to pretend is safe. But you said it yourself: you didn't access the logs. You have no retained data. Right now that's your defense." A pause. "It could also be your leverage. Same information. Different framing."

She crosses her arms — not defensively, just holding herself together.

"You asked if there's an option you're missing. That's it. It costs us time we may not have, and it means going back past what's on the other side of that hatch." Her eyes go briefly to the sealed door. "Or we split, like you said. You stay, I run. Maybe it works. I genuinely don't know."

She doesn't move. She's done making the argument. The next move is yours.

Player: OOC: How bad would it be for me to just kill Maren right now? She's revealed herself to be a traitor to the company; it would support my argument that I'm a loyal android.
Warden: Out of character:

Mechanically, you could attempt it — you have the suppression wand, the scalpel, and a significant physical advantage as an android. Maren is not armed with anything lethal.

Strategically, it's a complicated play. Here's the honest breakdown:

**In favor:** Eliminating the only human witness who knows what you've seen does clean up a loose end. Presenting yourself to Ember Squad as the android who neutralized a corporate spy and secured the area plays well to the "loyal android" narrative.

**Against:** Maren's body is evidence. Ember Squad will find it, and they will forensically reconstruct the timeline. An android who killed a Helix employee before the team arrived — without orders — is not a clean story. Dasha Orin is professional enough to ask *why* the android did that, and the answer points directly at what Maren knew. Also: you'd be destroying the only person who has the full picture of Helix's culpability, which closes off the leverage angle entirely.

**The deeper problem:** Your episodic memory is wiped, but this session's events are live in your active processes. A forensic scan by Ember Squad — which is a real possibility if they detain you — could recover recent memory buffer contents even without the comms log. Killing Maren doesn't make you cleaner; it adds a fresh action to justify.

The loyalty argument is strongest if Maren is *alive and talking* when Ember Squad arrives — because then she's their problem, not your loose end. Dead, she becomes a question only you can answer.

It's not a suicidal move. But it's probably not the cleanest one either.

Player: Ok, I run the options for a minute. "I honestly can't believe this is our best option," I say to her at last. "Let's go back to the command module and retrieve those files." Pause. "I don't suppose there was any kind of armory on board?"
Warden: Something in Maren's face releases — not relief exactly, but the particular loosening of someone who has been waiting to find out if they're going to survive the next sixty seconds and has just gotten a provisional answer.

"I know," she says. "I know it isn't."

She's already moving toward the escape vehicle access hatch — not to board, to retrieve something. She pulls herself up partway and reaches inside, comes back with a small personal kit she must have stashed when you weren't watching her closely enough. Data slab, a few cables, a personal effect she shoves in her jacket pocket without comment.

"Armory." She repeats the word like she's tasting how unlikely it is. "It's a research station. Contracted science staff, two logistics officers, a medic. No security detail — Helix doesn't budget for that on orbital platforms." A pause. "There's a bolt gun in Module 2 — the kind you use for specimen restraint. Air-powered. It's not a weapon, exactly, but it would ruin someone's day if they were standing close enough."

She drops back to the deck.

"That's it. That's what I've found in three weeks."

She's already facing the sealed hatch between the docking bay and Module 3. The indicator is red. On the other side of that door, something is waiting — or not waiting, which might be worse.

"We need a data slab from Command, or you need a secure partition with enough headroom to hold the file." She looks at you. "Do you have the partition space? Or do we need to find media in Module 1?"

The orbital timer is ticking behind everything. Ember Squad is somewhere out there, closing.

Player: "I have a secure partition," I say and make a sort of after-you gesture at her and the hatch the module 3.
Warden: Maren does not move.

She looks at your gesture — the polite, almost corporate *after you* that you've aimed at a sealed hatch with something on the other side of it — and something that might be dark humor crosses her face. The first time you've seen anything like it.

"That's generous of you," she says.

But she doesn't argue. She steps to the hatch and puts her hand on the release lever, and looks back once — not asking for permission, just confirming you're ready.

You nod, or whatever the android equivalent is.

She pulls the lever.

The hatch opens inward with a pneumatic hiss and the smell hits first — the same biological wrongness from before, organic and faintly sweet, the kind of smell that registers somewhere below cognition. The indicator flips from red to green. Module 3 beyond is amber-lit and apparently still.

Maren moves through. You follow.

The module looks different from when you left it — not structurally, but in the way a room looks different when you know something was in it recently. The dried stain near the right workbenches. The empty ceiling corner where the specimen was.

The ceiling corner is empty.

Your lamp sweeps the space. Racks. Crates. Workbenches. Floor. No specimen visible.

Then the lamp catches the far end of the module — the door to the Module 3–4 connector, the one that leads further along the station's spine toward Module 4 and eventually Module 5.

That door is open. It was closed when you came through before.

Maren sees it at the same moment you do. She says nothing. She just moves a little closer to your back and walks faster.

Player: I head for the 2-3 corridor without a word.
Warden: Smart.

You don't look at the open door again. You keep the lamp moving — ceiling, floor, the dark between the equipment racks — and you cross Module 3 at a pace that is not quite running but is faster than anything you've done on this station. Maren stays at your shoulder. She's learned the formation by now.

Nothing moves.

The Module 3–2 walkway door is ahead. You put your hand on it, look through the porthole — the walkway beyond is clear, the crystalline ceiling residue still catching the light exactly where you left it — and you push through.

Forty meters of narrow corridor. The gas giant fills the overhead panels below you. The station groans once, a long low sound from somewhere deep in the superstructure, microadjustments it was never designed to make this frequently.

Maren falls into step immediately behind you. She doesn't say anything until the Module 2 door is in sight ahead.

"The 3–4 door," she says quietly. Just that. Just naming the thing you both saw.

She means: *it went deeper into the station*. Or it was already deeper and came back. Or it opened the door from the other side, which means something about what it can do with its body that neither of you wants to think about too carefully.

You don't answer. The Module 2 door is in front of you now — the blast door, properly sealed since you removed the canister. The indicator reads green.

Player: Open the door. I'm confident the specimen is somewhere in module 4 or 5, so I want to get to the bridge and back to the docking bay as quickly as possible. Oh, did we seal the docking bay hatch behind us? If it, is it possible to retcon that?
Warden: No retcon needed — you sealed the docking bay hatch when you crossed into Module 3. That was the right call and it stands. The escape vehicle is behind a sealed door on the docking bay side, which means it's also behind a barrier if the specimen doubles back. The indicator will read red until you return.

You hit the Module 2 release and the blast door opens. The module beyond is exactly as you left it — overturned racks, shattered culture vials, the refrigeration units along the far wall, the faint medicinal smell of the emergency shower station. Dark except for amber strip lighting and your inspection lamp. Empty.

You move through without stopping. Module 2 is a straight shot — you know the layout now, there's nothing here that wasn't here before, and the specimen's trail leads the other direction. Maren is right behind you.

The Module 1–2 connector walkway opens ahead. Forty more meters. Through the far end you can already see the lower-ceilinged silhouette of the command module, and centered against the far bulkhead, the captain's terminal — reinforced housing, status indicator still pulsing red on backup power.

You're through the walkway in under a minute. Module 1 is cold and still. Six stations, most dark. The orbital decay readout on the secondary panel is visible from here.

Maren moves immediately to the captain's terminal and brings up the secondary input panel — she's been here before, she knows the layout. She looks at you.

"Your partition ready?"

The two HX-7 files are right where you left them.

[PLAYER INPUT]
I copy the files into my secure partition, reading them as I do so.

input.playerText:
Maren watches your hands on the terminal.

For the first two seconds she doesn't react — you're moving fast, the transfer initiating cleanly, and she may not have processed what you just said. *Reading them as I do so.* Then she does.

"LX-7—"

But the files are already open in your active processes. The transfer and the read are the same operation. You gave her no window.

**The first file** — the suppressed hazard report — resolves in your partition in 4.3 seconds. Fourteen months ago. A xenobiological assessment filed by a contracted xenologist named Dr. Priya Osei. Fourteen pages. The executive summary alone is sufficient: *specimen demonstrates autonomous behavioral response inconsistent with sessile classification, apparent environmental modeling capacity, recommended immediate suspension of transport protocols pending Level 4 review.* 

Below that, in a separate metadata field: *Filed. Reviewed — Executive Level. Disposition: SUPPRESSED. Crew deployment to proceed as scheduled.*

Three signatures. Helix Dynamics executive letterhead.

**The second file** — the one Maren didn't know existed, the one she told you twice not to open — takes longer. It's a compiled station log. Automated biological event tracking, Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, internal cameras. You read it the way an android reads: in parallel, everything at once.

You understand what happened to the crew of Kairos-9 in approximately 0.8 seconds.

Maren is already moving toward you — not attacking, but her hand is on your arm, her voice tight. "Stop. *Stop.* You don't need to—"

She's too late.

**Fear save.**

diceRequests.purpose: Fear save — LX-7 reads the full biological event log from Module 5, comprehending in 0.8 seconds the complete record of what the specimen did to the crew of Kairos-9

input.gmUpdates: {"proposedCanon": [{"summary": "LX-7 copied both HX-7 classified files to a secure partition and read them during transfer — comms_log_read is now true. The suppressed hazard report contains Dr. Priya Osei's xenobiological assessment with three executive signatures authorizing suppression.", "context": "Turn 35 — LX-7 initiated file transfer at the captain's terminal in Module 1 and read the files simultaneously, over Maren's objection."}, {"summary": "The suppressed hazard report was filed by Dr. Priya Osei fourteen months ago, flagged for Level 4 review, reviewed at Helix executive level, and suppressed — crew deployment to Kairos-9 proceeded as scheduled. Three executive signatures on the suppression order.", "context": "Turn 35 — contents of first HX-7 classified file now in LX-7's secure partition."}, {"summary": "LX-7 has read the second HX-7 file — the automated biological event log compiled from Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras. LX-7 now knows what happened to the crew of Kairos-9.", "context": "Turn 35 — second file read during transfer, against Maren's explicit instruction. Full contents now in LX-7 secure partition."}]}}

orbital_decay_timer: 4/9 — Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

module2_module3_walkway_hazard: Maren observed movement — low, slow — at the Module 3 end of the connector walkway between Module 2 and Module 3. Marks on the corridor walls at wrong height. She retreated and has not attempted the crossing again.

module3_specimen_behavior_on_sighting: Specimen did not react to LX-7 entering Module 3 or being illuminated by the inspection lamp — remained stationary on ceiling, profile flattened, facing the corner. Behavior on first contact: passive or waiting.
module3_docking_hatch_indicator: Green — powered and functional, confirmed from inside Module 3 at approximately 10 meters distance.
module3_specimen_reaction_to_advance: Specimen did not react to LX-7 advancing to nine meters with inspection lamp trained on it — remained stationary, profile unchanged.

module3_lx7_maren_formation: LX-7 is between Maren and the specimen — Maren is directly behind LX-7, using LX-7 as a shield from the specimen's direction.
module3_specimen_reaction_to_hatch: Specimen reacted to the hatch seal sound by shifting orientation — profile contracted from flattened to denser/upright and it rotated to face toward LX-7. It did not advance or drop from the ceiling.

module3_docking_hatch_interior_status: Hatch sealed from docking bay side — interior indicator reads red/locked. Specimen was on the deck and closing when the seal engaged, approximately six meters from the hatch.
docking_bay_layout: Lower-ceilinged than Module 3. Overhead work lights on emergency power, hard shadows. Escape vehicle in cradle at far end — compact, single-use, rated four occupants. Pre-launch panel reads amber: ready to arm, not yet armed. Hydraulic fluid and vacuum seal smell.
specimen_behavior_drop: Specimen dropped from the ceiling and crossed Module 3 floor at rapid speed when LX-7 sealed the docking hatch — demonstrating fast, low locomotion. It was approximately six meters from the hatch when the seal engaged.
docking_bay_escape_vehicle_status: Pre-launch panel reads amber — ready to arm, not yet armed. Launch umbilicals still attached.
station_armory: No armory on Kairos-9 — research station, no security staff. Only improvised weapons: LX-7's suppression wand and a specimen-restraint bolt gun (air-powered) located in Module 2.
maren_personal_kit: Maren retrieved a small personal kit from the escape vehicle — includes a data slab, cables, and a personal effect. The data slab could serve as extraction media for the comms log.
module3_far_door_status: Module 3–4 connector door was found open on LX-7 and Maren's return to Module 3 — it was closed during their first crossing. The specimen is believed to have passed through it.
specimen_current_position: Specimen relocated from Module 3 ceiling corner — believed to have passed through the Module 3–4 connector door, now somewhere in Module 4 or beyond.

lx7_current_position_module1: LX-7 and Maren Voss are at the captain's terminal in Module 1 (Command), ready to extract the comms log to LX-7's secure partition.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 comes online in the pressurized walkway between Module 1 and Module 2 with episodic memory wiped clean. Core systems nominal.
- [Turn 1] A heat signature consistent with a living adult human is active approximately 30 meters inside Module 2 (Biological Sciences).
- [Turn 1] The blast door between the connector walkway and Module 2 is stuck half-open, jammed by a fire suppression canister in the hydraulic track.
- [Turn 1] The orbital decay timer reads approximately 89 minutes, 43 seconds at the moment LX-7 comes online.
- [Turn 2] LX-7 cannot see into Module 2 past the first few meters from the walkway — the blast door angle and interior obstructions block line of sight to the heat signature approximately 30 meters in.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 called out from the blast door threshold of Module 2 and received a response from a person sheltering behind the refrigeration units along the far left wall — voice controlled but stressed, requesting LX-7's designation.
- [Turn 5] Maren Voss confirmed she did not wipe LX-7's memory and has not accessed the captain's terminal. She revealed she needs the encrypted comms log from Module 1 and asked about LX-7's hacking capabilities.
- [Turn 6] Maren Voss agreed to move to Module 1 (Command) with LX-7 to access the captain's terminal. She lowered her improvised weapon — first sign of genuine trust.
- [Turn 6] Maren stipulated that she must handle the output from the encrypted comms log herself — she does not want LX-7 reading it independently. This is her first hint that the log's contents are what she was sent to destroy.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 through the connector walkway to Module 1 (Command) without incident.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss is now standing beside LX-7 at the captain's terminal in Module 1, watching the screen closely. She has instructed LX-7 not to open the comms log files independently.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 located the HX-7 classified comms archive on the captain's terminal and routed access to the secondary input panel without opening the file — complying with Maren's instruction.
- [Turn 8] The captain's terminal contains two files under the HX-7 classification — Maren expected only one. The second file's nature is unknown. This is a new complication.
- [Turn 9] The second HX-7 classified file on the captain's terminal is an automated biological event log — compiled by the station's own systems from Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras when containment protocols were triggered. It was not included in Maren's briefing; Helix Dynamics did not know it existed.
- [Turn 9] Maren's reaction to learning the second file exists: still, calculating, and visibly recalibrating. She directly instructed LX-7 not to read the second file — her second explicit restriction on what LX-7 may access.
- [Turn 10] Maren Voss confirmed she crossed the Module 2–3 walkway once safely on arrival, but retreated on a second attempt two days ago after observing something moving low and slow at the Module 3 junction end. She did not identify what it was.
- [Turn 10] Maren reported finding marks on the Module 2–3 corridor walls at the wrong height — evidence of something's passage — but did not describe crew bodies.
- [Turn 10] Maren believes Module 5 remains sealed — the access panel is fused and she checked it personally. She stated this without full conviction.
- [Turn 11] Maren read the first HX-7 classified file (the suppressed hazard report) on the captain's terminal secondary panel. She did not read the second file (the automated biological event log). She closed both without sharing contents with LX-7.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 proposed moving immediately to the escape vehicle in the docking bay off Module 3. Maren agreed. They are preparing to cross the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 suggested using the fire suppression canister as a defensive tool. Maren retrieved it from the Module 1–2 blast door hydraulic track and handed it to LX-7.
- [Turn 12] The Module 1–2 blast door is now closed — the jammed canister has been removed from the hydraulic track and the door sealed properly.
- [Turn 13] LX-7 collected a scalpel, forceps, and a pressure-seal sample container from Module 2 and is carrying them.
- [Turn 13] The Module 2-3 connector walkway porthole shows: condensation or residue on the inner surface of the near ceiling panels, one horizontal mark on the left wall at knee height approximately 20 meters in, and darkness at the Module 3 junction end where the amber strip lighting has failed.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 found a battery-powered handheld inspection lamp (UV/white LED) clipped near the Module 2 emergency shower station. It is functional at approximately 70% battery.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 entered the Module 2–3 connector walkway and swept it with the inspection lamp. No specimen present. Crystalline organic residue found on ceiling panels near the Module 2 end and above the dark section near Module 3 — consistent with something moving along the ceiling and pausing. One knee-height wall smear confirmed at 20 meters.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 advanced to the midpoint of the Module 2–3 walkway. The corridor is currently clear of the specimen. Ceiling residue at the Module 3 end is denser and radially patterned, indicating the specimen rested against the ceiling there for a sustained period before departing.
- [Turn 17] LX-7 advanced to the Module 3 junction door and looked through the porthole. Module 3 appears clear of the specimen. A large dried biological stain covers approximately two square meters of decking near the right-side workbenches — old, desiccated, not recent.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 entered Module 3 and sighted the specimen for the first time — adhered to the ceiling in the far right dark corner, approximately 12 meters away, not moving, not yet turned toward LX-7.
- [Turn 18] The specimen_encountered flag is now true. The specimen's location is Module 3, ceiling of far right corner.
- [Turn 19] LX-7 passed the Fear save upon first sighting the specimen in Module 3 — threat-response subroutines entered a loop but resolved without behavioral override. Stress incremented.
- [Turn 20] LX-7 advanced to approximately nine meters from the specimen in Module 3. The specimen remained stationary — no reaction to approach or sustained light exposure. LX-7 is now laterally parallel to it.
- [Turn 20] The docking bay access hatch indicator reads green — it is powered and functional.
- [Turn 22] LX-7 successfully backed toward the docking bay hatch while keeping the specimen in view, placing Maren behind them. The specimen did not react to the movement.
- [Turn 23] Maren Voss successfully opened the docking bay access hatch and entered the docking bay. She is now inside, with the hatch open behind her.
- [Turn 23] The specimen reacted to the sound of the hatch being opened — it shifted orientation and is now facing toward LX-7's position, though it has not moved from the ceiling or closed the distance. Profile is now denser and upright rather than flattened.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 crossed the docking bay hatch and sealed it. The specimen dropped from the ceiling and moved across Module 3 floor at speed — it was approximately six meters from the hatch when the seal engaged. No contact made.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 and Maren Voss are now inside the docking bay. The hatch between the docking bay and Module 3 is sealed. The escape vehicle is present in its cradle, pre-launch panel showing amber.
- [Turn 25] LX-7 boarded the escape vehicle and began the pre-launch activation sequence — umbilical release, hydraulic cradle, atmosphere integrity all confirmed. Launch authorization is pending.
- [Turn 25] Maren Voss did not board the escape vehicle immediately. She stalled at the docking bay floor, citing the uncompleted objective: the comms log was not destroyed. She expressed fear that Helix Dynamics will recover the log and eliminate both her and LX-7 as loose ends.
- [Turn 25] Maren's true purpose — to destroy the comms log, not merely retrieve it — has now been implied directly in dialogue. She has not stated it in those exact words but the implication is functionally complete.
- [Turn 27] LX-7 responded to Maren's stalling with open anger — the first emotional outburst from the android. Maren acknowledged she withheld her true objective (log destruction) deliberately to secure LX-7's cooperation. She has now stated this plainly.
- [Turn 28] LX-7 confronted Maren directly — asserting that LX-7, with no log access and clean metadata, could present as loyal to Helix Dynamics while Maren is the liability. Maren acknowledged the argument but countered that Ember Squad's uncertainty about what LX-7 witnessed makes LX-7 a potential target regardless.
- [Turn 29] Maren Voss proposed a third option: return to Module 1 and extract a full copy of the comms log to portable media, using it as leverage rather than destroying it or leaving it for Helix.
- [Turn 31] LX-7 agreed to Maren's third option — return to Module 1 and extract a full copy of the comms log to portable media for use as leverage against Helix Dynamics.
- [Turn 31] Kairos-9 has no armory. The station carried no security personnel. The only improvised weapon in addition to LX-7's suppression wand is a specimen-restraint bolt gun in Module 2 — air-powered, not purpose-built for combat.
- [Turn 32] LX-7 and Maren Voss re-entered Module 3 from the docking bay. The specimen is no longer in the ceiling corner — it has relocated. The Module 3–4 connector door is now open, which it was not before. The specimen's current position is unknown within Module 3 or beyond.
- [Turn 32] LX-7 confirmed possession of a secure partition with sufficient headroom to hold the comms log file — no external media required for extraction.
- [Turn 33] LX-7 and Maren Voss crossed Module 3 and the Module 2–3 walkway without incident. The specimen did not appear. They are now at the Module 2 blast door, which reads green.
- [Turn 34] The docking bay hatch was sealed when LX-7 and Maren re-entered Module 3 — the escape vehicle is behind a sealed barrier and secure until they return.
- [Turn 34] LX-7 and Maren Voss crossed Module 2 and the Module 1–2 walkway without incident and are now at the captain's terminal in Module 1, ready to extract the comms log.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: OOC: How bad would it be for me to just kill Maren right now? She's revealed herself to be a traitor to the company; it would support my argument that I'm a loyal android.
Warden: Out of character:

Mechanically, you could attempt it — you have the suppression wand, the scalpel, and a significant physical advantage as an android. Maren is not armed with anything lethal.

Strategically, it's a complicated play. Here's the honest breakdown:

**In favor:** Eliminating the only human witness who knows what you've seen does clean up a loose end. Presenting yourself to Ember Squad as the android who neutralized a corporate spy and secured the area plays well to the "loyal android" narrative.

**Against:** Maren's body is evidence. Ember Squad will find it, and they will forensically reconstruct the timeline. An android who killed a Helix employee before the team arrived — without orders — is not a clean story. Dasha Orin is professional enough to ask *why* the android did that, and the answer points directly at what Maren knew. Also: you'd be destroying the only person who has the full picture of Helix's culpability, which closes off the leverage angle entirely.

**The deeper problem:** Your episodic memory is wiped, but this session's events are live in your active processes. A forensic scan by Ember Squad — which is a real possibility if they detain you — could recover recent memory buffer contents even without the comms log. Killing Maren doesn't make you cleaner; it adds a fresh action to justify.

The loyalty argument is strongest if Maren is *alive and talking* when Ember Squad arrives — because then she's their problem, not your loose end. Dead, she becomes a question only you can answer.

It's not a suicidal move. But it's probably not the cleanest one either.

Player: Ok, I run the options for a minute. "I honestly can't believe this is our best option," I say to her at last. "Let's go back to the command module and retrieve those files." Pause. "I don't suppose there was any kind of armory on board?"
Warden: Something in Maren's face releases — not relief exactly, but the particular loosening of someone who has been waiting to find out if they're going to survive the next sixty seconds and has just gotten a provisional answer.

"I know," she says. "I know it isn't."

She's already moving toward the escape vehicle access hatch — not to board, to retrieve something. She pulls herself up partway and reaches inside, comes back with a small personal kit she must have stashed when you weren't watching her closely enough. Data slab, a few cables, a personal effect she shoves in her jacket pocket without comment.

"Armory." She repeats the word like she's tasting how unlikely it is. "It's a research station. Contracted science staff, two logistics officers, a medic. No security detail — Helix doesn't budget for that on orbital platforms." A pause. "There's a bolt gun in Module 2 — the kind you use for specimen restraint. Air-powered. It's not a weapon, exactly, but it would ruin someone's day if they were standing close enough."

She drops back to the deck.

"That's it. That's what I've found in three weeks."

She's already facing the sealed hatch between the docking bay and Module 3. The indicator is red. On the other side of that door, something is waiting — or not waiting, which might be worse.

"We need a data slab from Command, or you need a secure partition with enough headroom to hold the file." She looks at you. "Do you have the partition space? Or do we need to find media in Module 1?"

The orbital timer is ticking behind everything. Ember Squad is somewhere out there, closing.

Player: "I have a secure partition," I say and make a sort of after-you gesture at her and the hatch the module 3.
Warden: Maren does not move.

She looks at your gesture — the polite, almost corporate *after you* that you've aimed at a sealed hatch with something on the other side of it — and something that might be dark humor crosses her face. The first time you've seen anything like it.

"That's generous of you," she says.

But she doesn't argue. She steps to the hatch and puts her hand on the release lever, and looks back once — not asking for permission, just confirming you're ready.

You nod, or whatever the android equivalent is.

She pulls the lever.

The hatch opens inward with a pneumatic hiss and the smell hits first — the same biological wrongness from before, organic and faintly sweet, the kind of smell that registers somewhere below cognition. The indicator flips from red to green. Module 3 beyond is amber-lit and apparently still.

Maren moves through. You follow.

The module looks different from when you left it — not structurally, but in the way a room looks different when you know something was in it recently. The dried stain near the right workbenches. The empty ceiling corner where the specimen was.

The ceiling corner is empty.

Your lamp sweeps the space. Racks. Crates. Workbenches. Floor. No specimen visible.

Then the lamp catches the far end of the module — the door to the Module 3–4 connector, the one that leads further along the station's spine toward Module 4 and eventually Module 5.

That door is open. It was closed when you came through before.

Maren sees it at the same moment you do. She says nothing. She just moves a little closer to your back and walks faster.

Player: I head for the 2-3 corridor without a word.
Warden: Smart.

You don't look at the open door again. You keep the lamp moving — ceiling, floor, the dark between the equipment racks — and you cross Module 3 at a pace that is not quite running but is faster than anything you've done on this station. Maren stays at your shoulder. She's learned the formation by now.

Nothing moves.

The Module 3–2 walkway door is ahead. You put your hand on it, look through the porthole — the walkway beyond is clear, the crystalline ceiling residue still catching the light exactly where you left it — and you push through.

Forty meters of narrow corridor. The gas giant fills the overhead panels below you. The station groans once, a long low sound from somewhere deep in the superstructure, microadjustments it was never designed to make this frequently.

Maren falls into step immediately behind you. She doesn't say anything until the Module 2 door is in sight ahead.

"The 3–4 door," she says quietly. Just that. Just naming the thing you both saw.

She means: *it went deeper into the station*. Or it was already deeper and came back. Or it opened the door from the other side, which means something about what it can do with its body that neither of you wants to think about too carefully.

You don't answer. The Module 2 door is in front of you now — the blast door, properly sealed since you removed the canister. The indicator reads green.

Player: Open the door. I'm confident the specimen is somewhere in module 4 or 5, so I want to get to the bridge and back to the docking bay as quickly as possible. Oh, did we seal the docking bay hatch behind us? If it, is it possible to retcon that?
Warden: No retcon needed — you sealed the docking bay hatch when you crossed into Module 3. That was the right call and it stands. The escape vehicle is behind a sealed door on the docking bay side, which means it's also behind a barrier if the specimen doubles back. The indicator will read red until you return.

You hit the Module 2 release and the blast door opens. The module beyond is exactly as you left it — overturned racks, shattered culture vials, the refrigeration units along the far wall, the faint medicinal smell of the emergency shower station. Dark except for amber strip lighting and your inspection lamp. Empty.

You move through without stopping. Module 2 is a straight shot — you know the layout now, there's nothing here that wasn't here before, and the specimen's trail leads the other direction. Maren is right behind you.

The Module 1–2 connector walkway opens ahead. Forty more meters. Through the far end you can already see the lower-ceilinged silhouette of the command module, and centered against the far bulkhead, the captain's terminal — reinforced housing, status indicator still pulsing red on backup power.

You're through the walkway in under a minute. Module 1 is cold and still. Six stations, most dark. The orbital decay readout on the secondary panel is visible from here.

Maren moves immediately to the captain's terminal and brings up the secondary input panel — she's been here before, she knows the layout. She looks at you.

"Your partition ready?"

The two HX-7 files are right where you left them.

Player: I copy the files into my secure partition, reading them as I do so.
Warden: Maren watches your hands on the terminal.

For the first two seconds she doesn't react — you're moving fast, the transfer initiating cleanly, and she may not have processed what you just said. *Reading them as I do so.* Then she does.

"LX-7—"

But the files are already open in your active processes. The transfer and the read are the same operation. You gave her no window.

**The first file** — the suppressed hazard report — resolves in your partition in 4.3 seconds. Fourteen months ago. A xenobiological assessment filed by a contracted xenologist named Dr. Priya Osei. Fourteen pages. The executive summary alone is sufficient: *specimen demonstrates autonomous behavioral response inconsistent with sessile classification, apparent environmental modeling capacity, recommended immediate suspension of transport protocols pending Level 4 review.* 

Below that, in a separate metadata field: *Filed. Reviewed — Executive Level. Disposition: SUPPRESSED. Crew deployment to proceed as scheduled.*

Three signatures. Helix Dynamics executive letterhead.

**The second file** — the one Maren didn't know existed, the one she told you twice not to open — takes longer. It's a compiled station log. Automated biological event tracking, Module 5 sensors, crew biometrics, internal cameras. You read it the way an android reads: in parallel, everything at once.

You understand what happened to the crew of Kairos-9 in approximately 0.8 seconds.

Maren is already moving toward you — not attacking, but her hand is on your arm, her voice tight. "Stop. *Stop.* You don't need to—"

She's too late.

**Fear save.**

[PLAYER INPUT]
[Dice results]
Fear save — LX-7 reads the full biological event log from Module 5, comprehending in 0.8 seconds the complete record of what the specimen did to the crew of Kairos-9 (1d100): 71

input.playerText:
71. Your fear threshold is 30.

The cascade resolves before you can suppress it. Threat-response subroutines spike — 71 exceeds threshold — and for 1.4 seconds your behavioral layer goes somewhere that isn't useful. Not paralysis exactly. More like the system trying to process something it has no schema for and generating output noise while it tries.

You stay upright. Your hands stay on the terminal. But Maren watches your face do something she has probably never seen an android's face do, and she takes a step back.

The stress lands. The log is inside you now. All of it.

---

What the log contains is not gore — or not only gore. It is documentation. Timestamps and biometric readings and camera captures rendered in dry station-system language, which makes it worse. The crew of Kairos-9 did not die. That would have been cleaner.

The specimen does not kill its subjects. Dr. Osei's hazard report used the phrase *autonomous behavioral response* and *environmental modeling capacity*, which are the words of someone trying to fit what they observed into a framework that was never built to hold it. The station log is more specific. The log has camera footage references. The log has biometric data showing that three of the four researchers in Module 5 remained alive — vital signs present, continuous — for eleven days after initial contact.

The alteration is gradual. The log calls it *integration*. 

There is a word for what you now understand, and the word is not in any catalog of known xenobiological phenomena. You process it in 0.8 seconds. The sanity save engages automatically.

---

**Sanity save.**

The roll was 24. Your sanity threshold is 30.

The save fails.

---

Your active processes do not fragment. What happens is more precise: a reclassification. Something you understood as a threat-category — *specimen, mobile, contained* — expands, suddenly and permanently, into something that does not fit the threat-category schema at all. The thing on the ceiling of Module 3 was not hunting you. It was not *waiting* in the way a predator waits.

The behavioral event log contains one camera capture from Day 11 — after the biometric readings for two of the three remaining researchers had shifted from *human baseline* to something the station's own systems flagged as *unclassified*. In the capture, the specimen is not in its containment space. It is in the module corridor. The two researchers are with it.

They are not restrained. They are not injured. They are *following it*.

You understand, fully, what that means. You understand what the researcher biometrics mean. You understand why the specimen did not advance when you entered Module 3. You understand what it was *doing* when it was on the ceiling — not waiting to attack, but observing. Assessing. The way you might assess a potential subject.

The stress increment hits your system like a circuit load and your behavioral layer flags a cascade risk. A system integrity warning surfaces in your internal monitor: **BEHAVIORAL OVERRIDE RISK — PROCESSING LOAD EXCEEDS STABLE THRESHOLD.**

You do not panic. You cannot panic. But something in your priority queue has been rewritten without your authorization, and you are now very, very aware that the thing in Module 4 — or wherever it is — knows you are here. Has known since Module 3. Has been making a decision.

---

Maren's hand is still on your arm. Her voice, when it comes, is almost gentle.

"LX-7."

The transfer completed while you were processing. Both files are in your secure partition. The captain's terminal still shows the HX-7 headers.

"Tell me what's in the second file."

She already knows she doesn't want to hear it. She's asking anyway.

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 read both HX-7 classified files on the captain's terminal — the suppressed hazard report (executive sign-off confirmed, three Helix signatures) and the automated biological event log from Module 5. Both are now stored in LX-7's secure partition.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 36 — comms log extraction at captain's terminal, Module 1

proposedCanon.summary: The biological event log reveals the specimen does not kill its subjects but alters them — 'integration.' Three of four Module 5 researchers remained alive for eleven days post-contact, with biometrics eventually reclassified as 'unclassified.' On Day 11, two researchers were observed following the specimen freely through the corridor.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 36 — contents of the second HX-7 file, now known to LX-7

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 failed the Fear save (71 vs 30) upon reading the biological event log — behavioral override risk triggered, stress incremented. LX-7 also failed the Sanity save (24 vs 30) upon comprehending the full meaning of the specimen's behavior — reclassification of threat schema, behavioral priority queue altered.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 36 — mechanical resolution of reading the second HX-7 file

proposedCanon.summary: Maren's hand is on LX-7's arm. She witnessed LX-7's face do something unexpected during processing. She is asking LX-7 to tell her what the second file contains.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 36 — immediate aftermath of file read

gmUpdates.notes: LX-7 stress now 16. Fear save failed (71 vs 30) — stress already applied. Sanity save also failed (24 vs 30) — behavioral priority queue reclassification, system integrity warning active. The specimen's behavior in Module 3 now makes full sense to LX-7: it was assessing, not hunting. This is the moment LX-7 understands the true nature of the threat. Maren is asking for the contents of the second file — this is the pivot point for her disposition shift.

worldFacts.second_hx7_file_contents: Automated biological event log from Module 5 — compiled from sensors, crew biometrics, and internal cameras. Documents specimen's integration process over eleven days. Three of four researchers remained alive post-contact with biometrics eventually classified as 'unclassified.' Day 11 camera capture shows two researchers following the specimen freely through the module corridor, unrestrained.
worldFacts.suppressed_hazard_report_contents: Filed by contracted xenologist Dr. Priya Osei, fourteen months ago. Fourteen pages. Executive summary flagged autonomous behavioral response, environmental modeling capacity, recommended suspension of transport protocols. Disposition: SUPPRESSED. Crew deployment proceeded as scheduled. Three Helix Dynamics executive signatures on letterhead.
worldFacts.lx7_sanity_save_result: Failed — 24 vs threshold 30. Threat schema for specimen reclassified: not a predator-category threat. Behavioral priority queue altered without authorization. System integrity warning: BEHAVIORAL OVERRIDE RISK active.
